rounds.question_text: One of these men was so broke he sold his dog for $40 — then bought it back for $15,000 after his first film was greenlit. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Stallone sold his bull mastiff Butkus before Rocky was greenlit. He was offered $350,000 for the script alone but refused unless he could star.
rounds.fun_fact: He bought the dog back from a guy outside a 7-Eleven for $15,000.

rounds.question_text: One of them won Mr. Universe at age 20, before acting in a single film. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Schwarzenegger won Mr. Universe at 20, then went on to win Mr. Olympia seven times before transitioning to acting.
rounds.fun_fact: He arrived in America at 21 speaking barely any English.

rounds.question_text: One of these men wrote his own breakout screenplay in three days and refused to sell it unless he could star. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Stallone wrote the Rocky screenplay in three and a half days. Studios offered up to $350,000 for the script, but he insisted on starring.
rounds.fun_fact: The film was made for just $1 million and grossed $225 million worldwide.

rounds.question_text: Who had the higher worldwide box office gross across their career?
rounds.explanation: Schwarzenegger's films grossed approximately $4.8 billion worldwide vs Stallone's $4.2 billion.
rounds.fun_fact: Both men appeared together in The Expendables franchise, finally sharing the screen after decades of rivalry.

rounds.question_text: One of them governed the world's fifth-largest economy. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Schwarzenegger served as Governor of California from 2003 to 2011. California's GDP would rank it as the world's fifth-largest economy.
rounds.fun_fact: He was nicknamed 'The Governator' — a mashup of Governor and Terminator.

rounds.question_text: "I'll be back" belongs to one of them. "Yo, Adrian!" belongs to the other. Who said "I'll be back"?
rounds.explanation: Schwarzenegger's "I'll be back" from The Terminator (1984) became one of the most quoted lines in cinema history.
rounds.fun_fact: He originally wanted to say 'I will be back' because the contraction felt unnatural with his accent. James Cameron insisted on 'I'll.'

rounds.question_text: One of them was inducted into the International Sports Hall of Fame for bodybuilding. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Schwarzenegger was inducted into the International Sports Hall of Fame in 2012 for his contributions to bodybuilding and fitness.
rounds.fun_fact: Stallone, meanwhile, was inducted into the International Boxing Hall of Fame in 2011 — for Rocky, not for actually boxing.

rounds.question_text: One of them earned a nickname that had nothing to do with speed — he was called 'The Professor' for his cerebral approach to racing. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Prost won by calculation, not aggression. He conserved tires and brakes, studied setups obsessively, and his mantra was to 'win as slowly as possible.'
rounds.fun_fact: He modelled his smooth driving style on Jackie Stewart and Jim Clark.

rounds.question_text: In the 1993 European Grand Prix at Donington, one of them started 5th, overtook four cars on the opening lap in a monsoon, and won by over a minute — lapping everyone up to second place. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Senna's opening lap at Donington 1993 is known as 'The Lap of the Gods.' He passed Schumacher, Wendlinger, Hill, and Prost before completing lap one.
rounds.fun_fact: He won the race by 1 minute and 23 seconds in a car that was not the fastest on the grid.

rounds.question_text: One of them held the all-time record of 65 career pole positions — a record that stood until Michael Schumacher broke it in 2006. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Senna took 65 poles from 161 race starts — nearly double Prost's 33 poles from 202 starts. He also held the record for most consecutive poles: 8 in a row.
rounds.fun_fact: Prost won more races (51 vs 41), but Senna was the master of single-lap qualifying speed.

rounds.question_text: At the 1989 Japanese Grand Prix, one of them turned into the other at the chicane to clinch the championship. Who caused the collision?
rounds.explanation: Prost turned in on Senna at Suzuka's Casio Triangle. Both cars tangled. Prost walked away. Senna restarted, won the race, but was disqualified for cutting the chicane.
rounds.fun_fact: One year later at the same track, Senna took revenge — deliberately driving into Prost at 160mph on lap one to clinch his second title.

rounds.question_text: One of them was fired by Ferrari before the final race of the 1991 season for publicly calling his car 'a truck.' Which one?
rounds.explanation: Prost drove for Ferrari in 1990-91. After criticizing the car's handling on Italian television, he was dismissed before the season finale in Adelaide.
rounds.fun_fact: The Italian press called him 'un traditore' — a traitor. He took a sabbatical year in 1992 before joining Williams.

rounds.question_text: One of them secretly donated millions to help impoverished children in his home country — the full scale was only discovered after his death. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Senna donated over $400 million during his lifetime. His sister Viviane founded the Instituto Ayrton Senna after his death, which has since helped over 15 million Brazilian children.
rounds.fun_fact: Brazil declared three days of national mourning. An estimated 3 million people lined the streets of São Paulo for the funeral procession.

rounds.question_text: Who won more World Championships?
rounds.explanation: Prost won 4 World Championships (1985, 1986, 1989, 1993) versus Senna's 3 (1988, 1990, 1991). At his final race in Adelaide 1993, Senna invited Prost onto the top step of the podium and embraced him.
rounds.fun_fact: Most people remember Senna as the greater driver. But Prost has the numbers. This debate has no end.

rounds.question_text: Before becoming famous, one of these two was a celebrity bodyguard — protecting the likes of Muhammad Ali, Michael Jackson, and Diana Ross. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mr. T worked as a bouncer at Chicago's Dingbats Discotheque, then became a celebrity bodyguard for nearly a decade. He claims to have been in over 200 fights as a bouncer.
rounds.fun_fact: Stallone discovered him on NBC's 'America's Toughest Bouncer' competition and cast him in Rocky III on the spot.

rounds.question_text: One of them shared a flat in 1970s Paris with Jerry Hall and Jessica Lange — all three were models at the same agency. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones, Jerry Hall, and Jessica Lange were all among the first models at the Euro Planning agency in Paris. Jones had moved there in 1970 and modeled for Yves Saint Laurent and Kenzo.
rounds.fun_fact: Before Paris, Jones had lived in hippie communes in Philadelphia and hitched rides with a motorcycle gang.

rounds.question_text: Both are famous for extreme hairstyles. One got their mohawk from a photo of Mandinka warriors in National Geographic. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mr. T was inspired by a 1977 National Geographic photo of Mandinka warriors. He specifically clarified it was a tribute to African warrior tradition, not Native American culture.
rounds.fun_fact: Grace Jones's iconic flattop was shaped by her partner and photographer Jean-Paul Goude, who also created her androgynous visual identity.

rounds.question_text: One of them played a Bond villain who possesses superhuman strength — and then switches sides and sacrifices herself to save Silicon Valley. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones played May Day in A View to a Kill (1985), opposite Christopher Walken as Max Zorin. She's one of the few Bond villains who becomes an ally by the end.
rounds.fun_fact: Roger Moore later said she genuinely scared him on set. He was 57. She didn't care.

rounds.question_text: One of them stopped wearing their signature gold chains after Hurricane Katrina, saying 'It would be a sin against God for me to wear my gold when so many people lost everything.' Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mr. T permanently retired his gold chains after helping with cleanup in New Orleans in 2005. As a born-again Christian, he said the display felt wrong in the face of such suffering.
rounds.fun_fact: The chains originally started as a practical move — as a bouncer, he'd collect jewelry left behind by patrons after fights.

rounds.question_text: One of them slapped a TV host on live television because he kept turning his back during the interview. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones slapped Russell Harty on his BBC chat show in November 1980. Ratings soared. Harty later joked his obituary headline would read 'Grace Jones Man Dead.'
rounds.fun_fact: She can still hula-hoop through an entire live performance of 'Slave to the Rhythm' in her late 70s. She did it at the Queen's Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

rounds.question_text: One of them legally changed their name so that 'the first word out of anybody's mouth is Mr. — out of a sign of respect.' Which one?
rounds.explanation: Born Laurence Tureaud, he changed his name at age 18 after watching his father be called 'boy.' He said: 'My first name is Mr., my middle name is period, my last name is T.'
rounds.fun_fact: Even his own mother called him Mr. T.

rounds.question_text: One of these shows featured a 'Ferrari' that was actually a replica built on a Chevrolet Corvette chassis. Ferrari was so embarrassed they donated two real cars to replace it. Which show?
rounds.explanation: Miami Vice's black 'Ferrari Daytona Spyder' was a kit car by McBurnie Coachcraft on a Corvette C3 chassis. Ferrari North America donated two white Testarossas on the condition the fakes be destroyed on-screen.
rounds.fun_fact: The real Testarossas were originally black — repainted white for the show's pastel aesthetic.

rounds.question_text: One of these actors was cast as Indiana Jones but had to turn it down because his network refused to release him from his TV contract. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Steven Spielberg and George Lucas offered Tom Selleck the role. CBS refused to release him from Magnum, P.I. — their highest-testing pilot. Harrison Ford got the whip instead.
rounds.fun_fact: Selleck later said: 'When I signed the deal for Magnum, it was the best thing that ever happened to me.'

rounds.question_text: One of these actors had a solo pop single reach #5 on the Billboard Hot 100. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson released 'Heartbeat' in 1986 on Epic Records. It peaked at #5 on the Hot 100 and charted across Europe — #3 in Germany and Austria.
rounds.fun_fact: Tom Selleck has no music career whatsoever.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in the highest-grossing film of 1987. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tom Selleck starred in Three Men and a Baby, which grossed $168 million domestically — the biggest film of 1987.
rounds.fun_fact: The sequel, Three Men and a Little Lady (1990), also did well but couldn't match the original.

rounds.question_text: One of these actors is the father of Fifty Shades of Grey star Dakota Johnson. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson and Melanie Griffith had daughter Dakota in 1989. Her maternal grandmother is Tippi Hedren, who starred in Hitchcock's The Birds.
rounds.fun_fact: Don Johnson married Melanie Griffith twice — in 1976 (lasted 6 months) and again in 1989 (lasted until 1996).

rounds.question_text: One of them met his wife backstage at a London West End production of Cats — she had no idea who he was because her TV had been stolen. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tom Selleck met Jillie Mack in 1983 when she was performing in Cats in London. They married in a secret ceremony near Lake Tahoe in 1987 and are still together.
rounds.fun_fact: They live on a 65-acre ranch in Ventura, California.

rounds.question_text: One of them won a Golden Globe but never an Emmy, despite being nominated twice for his iconic 80s show. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson won the Golden Globe for Best Actor in a TV Drama in 1986, but lost both his Emmy nominations (1985 and 1986) for Miami Vice.
rounds.fun_fact: Selleck won the Emmy for Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama in 1984 for Magnum, P.I.

rounds.question_text: One of these tennis legends was born on a US Air Force base in Wiesbaden, Germany. Which one?
rounds.explanation: McEnroe was born in Wiesbaden in 1959 while his father was stationed at the Air Force base. The family moved back to Queens, New York, when he was a child.
rounds.fun_fact: Borg was born in Södertälje, Sweden, and didn't pick up a tennis racket until his father won one in a table tennis tournament.

rounds.question_text: One of them won five consecutive Wimbledon titles — a feat no man had achieved in the Open Era. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Borg won Wimbledon from 1976 to 1980 — five straight years. Combined with his six French Open titles, he won 11 Grand Slam singles titles in total.
rounds.fun_fact: Despite dominating Wimbledon and Roland Garros, Borg never won the US Open — he lost the final four times.

rounds.question_text: One of them screamed 'You cannot be serious!' at a Wimbledon chair umpire in 1981 — a phrase that became the title of his autobiography. Which one?
rounds.explanation: McEnroe shouted at umpire Edward James during his first-round match at the 1981 Wimbledon Championships. He was docked a point and fined $1,500. Replays later suggested he was right.
rounds.fun_fact: He went on to win the tournament that year, beating Borg in the final.

rounds.question_text: In the legendary 1980 Wimbledon final, one of them won the fourth-set tiebreak 18-16 — saving five championship points — but still lost the match. Which one?
rounds.explanation: McEnroe won the 22-minute, 34-point tiebreak — one of the greatest passages of play in tennis history. But Borg won the fifth set 8-6 to claim his fifth consecutive Wimbledon.
rounds.fun_fact: The match lasted 3 hours and 53 minutes. It is widely considered the greatest tennis match ever played.

rounds.question_text: One of them retired at 26 at the peak of his fame, then attempted a comeback in 1991 using a wooden racket — and lost all 12 matches. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Borg retired in January 1983, citing burnout and loss of motivation. His 1991 comeback with his old Donnay wooden racket was a disaster — he went 0-12 before retiring permanently.
rounds.fun_fact: His coach said: 'Playing with a wooden racquet in 1991, it was like going to Iraq with a rifle.'

rounds.question_text: One of them became a fashion mogul with a Scandinavian underwear brand that still bears his name. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Björn Borg AB was established in 1984 and revolutionized men's underwear. Underwear accounts for 52% of company sales. He sold his full trademark rights for $18 million in 2006.
rounds.fun_fact: The brand operates in 7 European countries. Not bad for a guy who retired from tennis at 26.

rounds.question_text: One of them narrated all four seasons of Netflix's Never Have I Ever — a teen comedy created by Mindy Kaling. Which one?
rounds.explanation: McEnroe narrated every episode of Never Have I Ever from 2020 to 2023, playing a fictionalized version of himself as the inner voice of the teenage protagonist.
rounds.fun_fact: He is also one of tennis's most prominent TV commentators, working for ESPN, NBC, and BBC Wimbledon coverage.

tagline: God's Hand vs The King's Feet

rounds.question_text: One of them was named after American inventor Thomas Edison — his parents dropped the "i" from Edison. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pelé was born Edson Arantes do Nascimento. His parents named him after Thomas Edison but an error on the birth certificate changed the spelling.
rounds.fun_fact: The nickname "Pelé" came from mispronouncing a favourite goalkeeper's name at school. He hated the nickname at first.

rounds.question_text: In the span of four minutes during the 1986 World Cup, one of them scored both the most controversial goal AND the greatest goal in history — in the same match against England. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maradona scored the "Hand of God" (punching the ball past the goalkeeper) and then the "Goal of the Century" (dribbling past 5 English players) in the same quarterfinal match on June 22, 1986.
rounds.fun_fact: He said the first goal was scored "a little with the head of Maradona and a little with the hand of God."

rounds.question_text: One of them won the World Cup at age 17 — scoring twice in the final — becoming the youngest player to ever win the tournament. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pelé won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden at age 17, scoring two goals in the 5-2 final victory. He had started playing for Santos at 15 and for Brazil at 16.
rounds.fun_fact: He scored a hat-trick in the semi-final against France. The world had never seen a 17-year-old do anything like it.

rounds.question_text: One of them single-handedly lifted a southern Italian club to its first-ever league title, becoming a god in Naples. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maradona led Napoli to their first Scudetto in 1987 — a club from the poor south beating the wealthy northern giants. He won a second title in 1990.
rounds.fun_fact: Over a million people celebrated in the streets of Naples. The city had never won anything before Maradona arrived.

rounds.question_text: One of them won three World Cups — the only player in history to do so. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pelé won the World Cup in 1958, 1962, and 1970. No other player has ever won three. He scored 12 goals in 14 World Cup matches across his career.
rounds.fun_fact: The 1970 Brazil team is still widely considered the greatest football team ever assembled.

rounds.question_text: One of them came out of retirement at 34 to play for the New York Cosmos, sparking a soccer revolution in America. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pelé joined the New York Cosmos of the NASL in 1975, scoring 37 goals in 64 matches. His arrival put American soccer on the map.
rounds.fun_fact: Before Pelé, American stadiums were empty for soccer. After him, attendance tripled across the league.

rounds.question_text: One of them was banned from football for 15 months after failing a drug test during the 1994 World Cup. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maradona tested positive for ephedrine during the 1994 World Cup in the USA. He was expelled from the tournament and banned for 15 months. It was his last World Cup.
rounds.fun_fact: His wild-eyed celebration after scoring against Greece in that tournament — screaming into a camera — became one of football's most iconic and haunting images.

rounds.question_text: One of them arrived in New York City with $35 in her pocket and became the best-selling female recording artist of all time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Madonna moved to Manhattan in 1977 with $35, worked at Dunkin' Donuts and as a dancer. She went on to sell over 400 million records worldwide.
rounds.fun_fact: She studied briefly with the Alvin Ailey American Dance Theater before switching to music.

rounds.question_text: Before picking up a microphone, one of them worked as a Playboy Bunny waitress at New York's Playboy Club. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Debbie Harry worked as a Playboy Bunny at the New York Playboy Club from 1968 to 1973. She also waitressed at Max's Kansas City before co-founding Blondie in 1974.
rounds.fun_fact: She also worked as a go-go dancer in a New Jersey discothèque.

rounds.question_text: One of them had the first number-one single in US history to feature rap vocals — in 1981, years before rap went mainstream. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Blondie's "Rapture" hit #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 on March 28, 1981. It was also the first rap video aired on MTV when the channel launched later that year.
rounds.fun_fact: Debbie Harry name-checks graffiti artist Fab Five Freddy and DJ Grandmaster Flash in the rap verse.

rounds.question_text: One of them turned a cone-shaped bra designed by Jean Paul Gaultier into the most iconic costume in pop history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Madonna debuted the pink satin cone bra on the Blond Ambition Tour in 1990. Gaultier originally designed the cone shape for his teddy bear before adapting it for her.
rounds.fun_fact: The tour opened on April 13, 1990, in Chiba, Japan. The Vatican condemned it. Madonna didn't care.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a regular at CBGB alongside the Ramones, Talking Heads, and Television — helping define New York punk. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Blondie was a CBGB house band from 1974, performing alongside the Ramones, Talking Heads, and Television. The club on the Bowery incubated the entire punk and new wave movement.
rounds.fun_fact: Harry made CBGB's glamorous — she proved punk could have cheekbones.

rounds.question_text: One of them has sold over 400 million records worldwide — more than any female artist in history, certified by Guinness. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Madonna was recertified by Guinness World Records in 2023 as the best-selling female recording artist of all time. Only The Beatles, Elvis, and Michael Jackson have sold more.
rounds.fun_fact: She has had more #1 hits on the Billboard Dance Club chart than any artist in history — male or female.

rounds.question_text: One of them fronted a band whose name was also her nickname — and "Heart of Glass" made her the coolest blonde in New York. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Debbie Harry fronted Blondie — a name that doubled as her nickname. "Heart of Glass" (1979) became a worldwide #1 hit and crossed punk into disco territory.
rounds.fun_fact: She hated when people called the band "Blondie" thinking it was just her. The T-shirts read "Blondie is a group."

rounds.question_text: Both were born with very different names. One was born Reginald Kenneth Dwight. The other was born Farrokh Bulsara in Zanzibar. Who was Reginald?
rounds.explanation: Elton John was born Reginald Kenneth Dwight on March 25, 1947. He legally changed his name in 1972. Freddie Mercury was born Farrokh Bulsara in Zanzibar and adopted "Freddie" at boarding school in India.
rounds.fun_fact: Mercury's classmates gave him the nickname because they couldn't pronounce Farrokh.

rounds.question_text: One of them delivered what was voted the greatest live performance in rock history — a 21-minute set at Wembley in 1985 that included "Bohemian Rhapsody" and "We Are the Champions." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Freddie Mercury and Queen's Live Aid performance on July 13, 1985, was voted the greatest live performance ever in a 2005 industry poll. His sustained "Aaaaaay-o" call-and-response became known as "The Note Heard Round the World."
rounds.fun_fact: The 72,000-strong Wembley crowd sang in perfect unison. The BBC called it "the greatest display of community singing the old stadium had seen."

rounds.question_text: One of them recorded the best-selling single since charts began — a tribute performed only once, at a funeral in Westminster Abbey watched by 2.5 billion people. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Elton John's "Candle in the Wind 1997" for Princess Diana's funeral is the highest-selling single since charts began in the 1950s. It sold 658,000 copies on its first day in the UK alone.
rounds.fun_fact: He never performed the song publicly again after the funeral. Only Bing Crosby's "White Christmas" (1942) has sold more physical copies.

rounds.question_text: Scientists studied one of their voices and found he could vibrate his vocal cords at roughly four times the speed of a typical singer, explaining his extraordinary power. Which one?
rounds.explanation: A 2016 study by Austrian, Czech, and Swedish researchers analyzed Mercury's voice and found his vocal cords vibrated with remarkable speed and irregularity, producing his unique timbre and power.
rounds.fun_fact: Despite being known as a tenor, the researchers concluded he was actually a baritone who could sing in the tenor range.

rounds.question_text: One of them was knighted by Queen Elizabeth II and became "Sir" — for services to music and charity. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Elton John was knighted in 1998, becoming Sir Elton Hercules John. He received the honor for his contributions to music and his charitable work, particularly around AIDS awareness.
rounds.fun_fact: He is one of the few musicians to have achieved EGOT status — Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony.

rounds.question_text: After losing two friends to AIDS — including the other man in this matchup — one of them founded a charity that has raised over $600 million. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Elton John founded the Elton John AIDS Foundation in 1992 after losing Freddie Mercury and another close friend to AIDS within the same year. It has raised over $600 million.
rounds.fun_fact: John was among 35 close friends at Mercury's funeral on November 27, 1991.

rounds.question_text: One of them publicly confirmed his AIDS diagnosis only 24 hours before dying at age 45. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Freddie Mercury issued a public statement confirming his AIDS diagnosis on November 23, 1991. He died the following evening, November 24, at his home in Kensington.
rounds.fun_fact: He had kept his diagnosis private for years. His final statement read: "I felt it correct to keep this information private to protect the privacy of those around me."

tagline: Enter the Dragon vs Missing in Action

rounds.question_text: In Way of the Dragon (1972), these two fought inside the Roman Colosseum — the crew bribed officials and snuck in cameras because filming was illegal. Who won the fight on screen?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee's character defeats Chuck Norris's character Colt in the climactic fight. Lee personally choreographed every move. It is widely considered the greatest movie fight ever filmed.
rounds.fun_fact: The two were friends in real life and trained together. Norris later joked that he only agreed because Lee promised him he'd look good losing.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a real six-time Professional Middleweight Karate Champion with a competitive fight record of 65 wins and 5 losses. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Chuck Norris dominated professional karate tournaments from 1965 to 1970, winning virtually every major title including the World Professional Middleweight Championship.
rounds.fun_fact: He compiled wins over champions Joe Lewis, Arnold Urquidez, and Skipper Mullins. He was the real deal.

rounds.question_text: One of them was rejected by Hollywood for being "too Chinese" — losing the lead role in Kung Fu to David Carradine. So he went to Hong Kong and became the biggest movie star on earth. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee was passed over for the role of Kwai Chang Caine in the TV series Kung Fu, which went to non-Asian actor David Carradine. Frustrated, Lee returned to Hong Kong and made history.
rounds.fun_fact: His first Hong Kong film, The Big Boss, broke box office records across Asia. Hollywood came crawling back.

rounds.question_text: One of them became the internet's first major meme — absurd "facts" about his invincibility started on forums in 2005 and spread worldwide. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Chuck Norris Facts originated on Something Awful forums in 2005, initially about Vin Diesel before switching to Norris. A high school student named Ian Spector built a generator site that drew 10,000 users overnight.
rounds.fun_fact: Norris embraced it completely. He said his personal favourite was: "They once tried to carve Chuck Norris's face into Mount Rushmore. The granite wasn't hard enough."

rounds.question_text: One of them rejected all classical martial arts styles and created his own philosophy called Jeet Kune Do — "The Way of the Intercepting Fist." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee studied Wing Chun, boxing, fencing, and other arts, then rejected rigid styles in favor of taking what works from each. Jeet Kune Do was his personal martial arts philosophy, not a fixed system.
rounds.fun_fact: He said: "Absorb what is useful, discard what is useless, and add what is specifically your own."

rounds.question_text: One of them dedicated his Missing in Action film trilogy to his younger brother, who was killed in Vietnam while serving in the 101st Airborne Division. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Chuck Norris's brother Wieland was a private in the 101st Airborne, killed in June 1970 while on patrol near Firebase Ripcord in Vietnam. The Missing in Action films (1984-1988) were his tribute.
rounds.fun_fact: Norris served in the US Air Force before becoming a martial artist. Military service ran in the family.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at 32, and his most famous film was released six days after his death. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee died on July 20, 1973, in Hong Kong at age 32 from cerebral edema. Enter the Dragon premiered on July 26, 1973 — six days later — and became a global phenomenon.
rounds.fun_fact: He once said: "The key to immortality is first living a life worth remembering." He was right.

rounds.question_text: One of them won Wimbledon at 17 — the youngest men's singles champion in the tournament's history, and he was completely unseeded. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Boris Becker won Wimbledon in 1985 at 17 years and 7 months, beating Kevin Curren in four sets. He was the first unseeded player and the first German to win the title.
rounds.fun_fact: His diving volleys became his trademark — the press dubbed his acrobatic playing style "Becker's Boing."

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in a camera commercial with the tagline "Image is everything" — a phrase that haunted him for years because critics said he was all style and no substance. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Andre Agassi filmed the Canon Rebel camera ad in the Nevada desert, stepping out of a white Lamborghini and uttering the infamous line. It took three more years before he won his first Grand Slam.
rounds.fun_fact: He later called it the worst career decision he ever made. His autobiography "Open" was partly a response to the "image" label.

rounds.question_text: One of them wore a wig held together with pins during a Grand Slam final — and later said the fear of it falling off cost him the match. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Agassi wore a hairpiece in the 1990 French Open final against Andres Gomez. He spent the entire match terrified it would fall apart on live television. He lost. He revealed the secret in his 2009 autobiography.
rounds.fun_fact: His flowing lion-mane hair of the early 90s — one of the most iconic looks in sports — was completely fake.

rounds.question_text: One of them was sentenced to two and a half years in a British prison for hiding £2.5 million in assets during bankruptcy proceedings. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Boris Becker was sentenced in April 2022 for concealing assets during bankruptcy. He served eight months before being released and deported to Germany under a fast-track scheme for foreign prisoners.
rounds.fun_fact: The judge noted his 2002 suspended sentence for tax evasion in Germany as an aggravating factor — he hadn't learned.

rounds.question_text: One of them is the first male tennis player in history to win all four Grand Slam titles plus an Olympic gold medal across his career. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Agassi completed the Career Golden Slam by winning the 1999 French Open — the only Grand Slam he was missing. Combined with his 1996 Olympic gold in Atlanta, he achieved what no man had done before.
rounds.fun_fact: He won 8 Grand Slam titles total: 4 Australian Opens, 2 US Opens, 1 Wimbledon, 1 French Open.

rounds.question_text: One of them married the greatest female tennis player of all time — together they hold more Grand Slam singles titles than any couple in history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Andre Agassi married Steffi Graf in October 2001. Between them they hold 30 Grand Slam singles titles (his 8 + her 22). They are still married and live in Las Vegas.
rounds.fun_fact: They started dating after both won the French Open in 1999. She had also completed a Calendar Year Golden Slam in 1988 — the only player ever to do so.

rounds.question_text: One of them won 6 Grand Slam titles, including three Wimbledons. The other won 8. Who won 6?
rounds.explanation: Becker won 6 Grand Slam singles titles: Wimbledon (1985, 1986, 1989), Australian Open (1991, 1996), and US Open (1989). Agassi won 8 across all four majors.
rounds.fun_fact: Despite fewer titles, Becker reached 7 Wimbledon finals — the tournament was his kingdom.

rounds.question_text: One of them funded a record label with drug money — reportedly investing $6,000 to $10,000 from dealing crack cocaine in Compton. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eazy-E founded Ruthless Records in 1987 with money earned from drug dealing. By age 22, he had allegedly earned as much as $250,000 from selling drugs before deciding music was a better path.
rounds.fun_fact: His cousin being shot and killed was the turning point that pushed him from the streets into the studio.

rounds.question_text: One of them wrote most of N.W.A.'s lyrics — including "Straight Outta Compton" and "Fuck tha Police." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ice Cube was N.W.A.'s primary lyricist. He wrote the majority of the group's debut album Straight Outta Compton, the landmark record that defined West Coast gangsta rap.
rounds.fun_fact: When he left N.W.A., the group lost its main writer. Dr. Dre departed not long after.

rounds.question_text: One of them released "No Vaseline" — widely considered the greatest diss track in hip-hop history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ice Cube released "No Vaseline" in 1991 on his album Death Certificate, attacking Eazy-E and manager Jerry Heller for exploiting N.W.A. financially. The group never responded.
rounds.fun_fact: After the track dropped, N.W.A. effectively dissolved. Dr. Dre left citing the same financial complaints.

rounds.question_text: One of them co-wrote and starred in the 1995 comedy Friday, launching a major acting career with nearly 40 films. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ice Cube co-wrote Friday with DJ Pooh and starred as Craig Jones. The film launched a franchise and transformed him from gangsta rapper to comedic actor and producer.
rounds.fun_fact: He was 26 when Friday came out. He went on to star in the Barbershop and Are We There Yet? franchises.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the sole owner of Ruthless Records — a label that launched N.W.A., Bone Thugs-N-Harmony, and Above the Law. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eazy-E owned Ruthless Records outright, with Jerry Heller as his business partner/manager. The label was one of the most important in hip-hop history.
rounds.fun_fact: At its peak, Ruthless was generating $10 million a month in revenue.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a distinctive high-pitched nasal voice that became the most recognizable sound in gangsta rap — despite having almost no formal musical training. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eazy-E's raspy, high-pitched delivery was instantly recognizable and completely self-taught. He had no musical background before Dr. Dre and Ice Cube brought him into the studio.
rounds.fun_fact: Dr. Dre reportedly had to coach him through his first recordings line by line.

rounds.question_text: One of them died of AIDS-related complications at age 30 — just one month after being diagnosed. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eazy-E was diagnosed with HIV in February 1995 and died on March 26, 1995. His rapid decline shocked the hip-hop world and brought mainstream attention to AIDS awareness.
rounds.fun_fact: On his deathbed, he reconciled with Ice Cube and Dr. Dre. The beef died with him.

rounds.question_text: One of them led a trio from Detroit that became Motown's most successful act — with 12 number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Diana Ross led The Supremes, the most commercially successful Motown group ever. Their 12 number-one hits include "Where Did Our Love Go," "Stop! In the Name of Love," and "You Can't Hurry Love."
rounds.fun_fact: Combining her Supremes and solo hits, Ross has 18 number-one singles — the most for any female artist in US chart history.

rounds.question_text: One of them had seven consecutive number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100 — a record no other artist has ever matched. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Whitney Houston's first seven singles all reached number one, from "Saving All My Love for You" (1985) through "Where Do Broken Hearts Go" (1988). No artist before or since has done this.
rounds.fun_fact: Her label initially worried that her music was "too pop" for Black radio and "too Black" for pop radio. She conquered both.

rounds.question_text: One of them played Billie Holiday in a 1972 biographical film and was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actress. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Diana Ross starred in Lady Sings the Blues (1972) as jazz legend Billie Holiday. She received an Oscar nomination for Best Actress in her film debut.
rounds.fun_fact: The soundtrack album went to number one on the Billboard chart. Ross did her own singing throughout the film.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in a 1992 film whose soundtrack became the best-selling movie soundtrack of all time — over 45 million copies sold. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Whitney Houston starred in The Bodyguard (1992) alongside Kevin Costner. The soundtrack sold over 45 million copies worldwide, anchored by her cover of "I Will Always Love You."
rounds.fun_fact: "I Will Always Love You" was originally written and recorded by Dolly Parton in 1973. Houston's version spent 14 weeks at number one.

rounds.question_text: One of them recorded a cover of another artist's song that became the best-selling physical single by a woman in US history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Whitney Houston's version of Dolly Parton's "I Will Always Love You" was certified Diamond by the RIAA. It remains the best-selling physical single by a female artist in the United States.
rounds.fun_fact: Parton later said: "When Whitney did my song, I got enough money to buy Dollywood."

rounds.question_text: One of them was named the most successful female artist in UK chart history, based on a career tally spanning solo work and group hits. Which one?
rounds.explanation: In 1999, Diana Ross was officially named the most successful female chart artist in UK history, based on combined chart performance across The Supremes and her solo career.
rounds.fun_fact: She has been performing for over 60 years. Her career spans from Motown in the 1960s to sold-out arena tours in the 2020s.

rounds.question_text: One of them was honored at the Soul Train Music Awards as the female artist of the 1990s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Whitney Houston received the special honor at the 14th Soul Train Music Awards in March 2000, recognized as the defining female artist of the entire decade.
rounds.fun_fact: She sold over 200 million records worldwide. Only Madonna has sold more among female artists.

rounds.question_text: One of them appeared on the Forbes list of the world's richest people for seven consecutive years — with an estimated fortune of $30 billion at peak. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pablo Escobar was listed by Forbes from 1987 to 1993. In 1989, he was ranked 20th richest in the world with a reported $3 billion — though his son later said the real number was far higher.
rounds.fun_fact: At his peak, the Medellín Cartel supplied an estimated 80% of the world's cocaine.

rounds.question_text: One of them launched his empire with a magazine whose first issue featured Marilyn Monroe on the cover — despite never having met her or paid her for the photos. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Hugh Hefner launched Playboy in December 1953 with Monroe on the cover. He paid $500 for nude photos that had been taken years earlier. Monroe never consented to their use in his magazine.
rounds.fun_fact: The first issue sold over 50,000 copies at 50 cents each. Hefner was so unsure of success that he didn't even put a date on it.

rounds.question_text: One of them reportedly burned $2 million in cash to keep his daughter warm when she had hypothermia while the family was on the run. Which one?
rounds.explanation: According to Escobar's son Sebastián Marroquín, his father burned approximately $2 million in banknotes to make a fire when his daughter Manuela was suffering from hypothermia in a mountain hideout.
rounds.fun_fact: The Medellín Cartel reportedly spent $2,500 per month just on rubber bands to wrap their cash.

rounds.question_text: One of them lived in a 22,000-square-foot Gothic mansion with a zoo, a grotto, and a legendary pool that became the most famous party house in America. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Hugh Hefner bought the Playboy Mansion in Los Angeles in 1971 for $1 million. It featured 29 rooms, a famous grotto, and became the world's most notorious party venue. It sold for $100 million in 2016.
rounds.fun_fact: He lived there until his death in 2017, age 91. The sale condition was that he could keep living there.

rounds.question_text: One of them built a private estate with a zoo that included hippos, elephants, and giraffes imported illegally from Africa. The hippos escaped and now roam Colombia. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Escobar's Hacienda Nápoles estate included an exotic zoo. After his death, the hippos were left behind and bred. Colombia now has over 100 feral hippos — the largest population outside Africa.
rounds.fun_fact: They are considered an invasive species and a major ecological problem. Nobody knows how to deal with them.

rounds.question_text: One of them created a brand so iconic that the bunny logo became one of the most recognized symbols in the world. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Playboy bunny logo, designed by art director Art Paul in 1953, became one of the most famous brand symbols globally. It has been printed on everything from jewelry to jet planes.
rounds.fun_fact: Hefner chose a rabbit because he thought the bunny had a "humorous sexual connotation."

rounds.question_text: One of them was killed in a rooftop shootout with police at age 44, ending a manhunt that involved the DEA, Colombian military, and a vigilante group. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pablo Escobar was shot and killed on December 2, 1993, on a rooftop in the Los Olivos neighborhood of Medellín. He was 44 years old. The Search Bloc, a special unit backed by the US, tracked him via a phone call to his son.
rounds.fun_fact: His grave in Medellín remains one of the most visited sites in Colombia. Flowers are left daily.

tagline: Universal Soldier: Who's the Real One?

rounds.question_text: One of them performed a split between two reversing Volvo trucks in a 2013 commercial that became one of the most-watched ads in YouTube history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Van Damme performed "The Epic Split" for Volvo Trucks, filmed in a single take at an airport in Spain at sunrise. The ad demonstrated the precision of Volvo's Dynamic Steering system.
rounds.fun_fact: The video has been viewed over 100 million times. It was filmed with no wires or CGI — the split is real.

rounds.question_text: One of them holds a master's degree in chemical engineering and was awarded a Fulbright Scholarship to MIT — which he quit after two weeks to pursue acting. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dolph Lundgren earned a chemical engineering degree from KTH Royal Institute of Technology in Sweden and a master's from the University of Sydney. He was awarded a Fulbright to MIT in 1983 but left for Hollywood.
rounds.fun_fact: He speaks five languages: Swedish, English, German, French, and some Japanese and Spanish.

rounds.question_text: One of them drove a taxi and delivered pizzas in Hollywood while trying to break into acting. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Van Damme moved to Los Angeles in 1982 with little money and no English. He worked as a limo driver, pizza delivery man, carpet layer, and bouncer while hunting for his break.
rounds.fun_fact: He famously stalked producer Menahem Golan outside a restaurant and performed a spinning kick to get his attention. It worked — Golan cast him in Bloodsport.

rounds.question_text: One of them played Ivan Drago in Rocky IV — a Soviet boxer so terrifying that Sylvester Stallone was hospitalized after a real punch during filming. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dolph Lundgren played Captain Ivan Drago in Rocky IV (1985). Stallone asked Lundgren to hit him for real during filming. The punch was so hard that Stallone spent four days in intensive care with a swollen pericardium.
rounds.fun_fact: Lundgren's line "I must break you" became one of the most quoted lines in 80s action cinema.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in JCVD (2008) — a critically acclaimed semi-autobiographical film where he played a washed-up version of himself and delivered a six-minute monologue about his failed career. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Van Damme starred in JCVD, a Belgian-French film where he plays a fictionalized version of himself — broke, divorced, and stuck in a hostage situation. His raw, tearful monologue earned him the best reviews of his career.
rounds.fun_fact: Critics called it "revelatory." Roger Ebert wrote: "Who would have guessed Van Damme could act?"

rounds.question_text: Before acting, one of them worked as Grace Jones's personal bodyguard and bouncer — and briefly dated her. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dolph Lundgren was Grace Jones's bodyguard and boyfriend before breaking into film. She introduced him to the world of entertainment and helped him get the audition for Rocky IV.
rounds.fun_fact: Their relationship was well-documented by paparazzi. Andy Warhol photographed them together at Studio 54.

rounds.question_text: They co-starred in Universal Soldier (1992). One of them played the hero, the other the villain. Who was the villain?
rounds.explanation: Dolph Lundgren played Sergeant Andrew Scott, the psychopathic villain, while Van Damme played the hero Luc Deveraux. The film spawned multiple sequels pairing them again.
rounds.fun_fact: Reportedly, real tension existed between them during filming. Both men were competitive about who got more screen time.

rounds.question_text: One of them has fronted the same band for over 60 years — a group often called "the greatest rock and roll band in the world." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mick Jagger has been the lead singer of The Rolling Stones since 1962. The band's career spans over six decades with no permanent frontman change.
rounds.fun_fact: Rod Stewart was briefly in The Faces with Ronnie Wood — who later joined The Rolling Stones.

rounds.question_text: One of them spent 23 years building an elaborate model railway in his attic — a hobby he used to hide because he found it embarrassing. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Rod Stewart built a massive model railway called 'Grand Street and Three Rivers City,' set in 1940s America. He worked on it for 23 years and up to 5 hours a day, even booking extra hotel rooms on tour for his model work.
rounds.fun_fact: He finally revealed it in Railway Modeller magazine. He said: "I'm no longer embarrassed. I'm proud of it."

rounds.question_text: Both were knighted. One became Sir in 2003, the other in 2016. Who was knighted first?
rounds.explanation: Mick Jagger was knighted in December 2003 by Prince Charles for services to popular music. Rod Stewart was knighted in October 2016 by Prince William.
rounds.fun_fact: Keith Richards was furious about Jagger's knighthood, calling it "ludicrous to take one of those gongs from the Establishment."

rounds.question_text: One of them had a worldwide hit asking "Da Ya Think I'm Sexy?" — a song that hit #1 in the US and UK. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Rod Stewart released "Da Ya Think I'm Sexy?" in 1978. It spent four weeks at #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 and became one of the defining disco-era crossover hits.
rounds.fun_fact: He was accused of plagiarizing a Jorge Ben song. He ended up donating all royalties to UNICEF.

rounds.question_text: One of them has fathered eight children with five different women. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mick Jagger has eight children with five women, spanning from his eldest Karis (born 1970) to his youngest Deveraux (born 2016, when Jagger was 73).
rounds.fun_fact: Rod Stewart also has eight children — with five women. They're matched on this stat.

rounds.question_text: One of them has sold an estimated 250 million records with his band alone — not counting solo work. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Rolling Stones have sold an estimated 200-250 million records worldwide, making them one of the best-selling music artists of all time. This doesn't include Jagger's solo work.
rounds.fun_fact: Rod Stewart has sold around 120 million records total — combining solo and Faces work.

rounds.question_text: One of them publicly claimed to be the better singer, saying: "Mick's a fine blues singer, but technically not as good as me." Who said it?
rounds.explanation: Rod Stewart made this claim in interviews, positioning himself as the superior vocalist. The two have maintained a friendly rivalry for decades, often trading barbs in the press.
rounds.fun_fact: Jagger once invited Stewart to a party that Stewart later described in interviews with considerable detail. Their friendship has outlasted most of their marriages.

rounds.question_text: One of them was discovered when his cousin handed a demo cassette tape to the other. Which one was discovered?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg was introduced to Dr. Dre through Warren G, who gave Dre a cassette of Snoop's freestyle. Dre signed him immediately, and their first collaboration was the 1992 single "Deep Cover."
rounds.fun_fact: Warren G is Dr. Dre's step-brother. The whole thing was a family connection.

rounds.question_text: One of them produced The Chronic (1992) — the album that defined G-funk and West Coast hip-hop. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dr. Dre produced and performed on The Chronic, featuring Snoop Dogg heavily. The album pioneered the G-funk sound — laid-back synths, deep bass, and melodic hooks over gangsta lyrics.
rounds.fun_fact: Snoop appeared on so many tracks that people joked The Chronic was really a Snoop Dogg album with Dre producing.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a debut solo album that entered the Billboard 200 at number one with 806,000 copies sold in its first week. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg's Doggystyle (1993) debuted at #1 with 806,000 first-week sales, a record at the time. It featured "Gin and Juice" and "What's My Name?"
rounds.fun_fact: He recorded much of the album while facing a murder charge. He was acquitted in 1996.

rounds.question_text: One of them co-founded Beats Electronics, which Apple bought for $3 billion in 2014 — making him hip-hop's first billionaire. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dr. Dre co-founded Beats with Jimmy Iovine in 2008. Apple's $3 billion acquisition in 2014 made Dre the wealthiest figure in hip-hop history.
rounds.fun_fact: Dre and Iovine both joined Apple's executive team after the sale.

rounds.question_text: One of them became best friends with Martha Stewart — co-hosting a cooking show, attending the Olympics together, and becoming one of America's most unlikely celebrity duos. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg and Martha Stewart became friends after meeting on her show in 2008. They co-hosted Martha & Snoop's Potluck Dinner Party and attended the 2024 Paris Olympics together.
rounds.fun_fact: Stewart once said Snoop brings "a very different perspective to entertaining" than she does. No kidding.

rounds.question_text: One of them discovered and launched the careers of Eminem, 50 Cent, and Kendrick Lamar — arguably the three biggest rappers of three consecutive decades. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dr. Dre signed and produced debut albums for Eminem (1999), 50 Cent (2003), and Kendrick Lamar (2012). No other producer has launched three era-defining artists.
rounds.fun_fact: He has only released three solo albums in 30+ years. His influence comes from producing others.

rounds.question_text: One of them went from gangsta rapper to mainstream TV personality — coaching on The Voice, carrying the Olympic torch, and becoming a family-friendly brand. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg became an NBC commentator for the 2024 Paris Olympics, has coached on The Voice, and carries the Olympic torch. His transformation from gangsta rap to wholesome entertainment icon is one of pop culture's greatest pivots.
rounds.fun_fact: He also coaches youth football in Los Angeles. The league has produced multiple NFL players.

rounds.question_text: One of them was born Norma Jeane Mortenson and reinvented herself with a new name that became the most famous in Hollywood history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Marilyn Monroe was born Norma Jeane Mortenson on June 1, 1926. She changed her name when she began modeling, taking her mother's maiden name Monroe and the first name Marilyn, suggested by a studio executive.
rounds.fun_fact: She spent much of her childhood in foster homes and an orphanage before becoming the biggest movie star in the world.

rounds.question_text: One of them emerged from the Caribbean Sea in a white bikini in 1962 — creating one of the most iconic moments in cinema. The bikini later sold for $61,500 at auction. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ursula Andress rose from the sea as Honey Ryder in Dr. No (1962), the first James Bond film. The white bikini with a diving knife became one of the most famous costumes in film history.
rounds.fun_fact: She won the Golden Globe for New Star of the Year in 1964 for the role.

rounds.question_text: One of them sang "Happy Birthday" to the President of the United States in a dress so tight it had to be sewn onto her body. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Marilyn Monroe serenaded President John F. Kennedy on May 19, 1962, at Madison Square Garden. Her breathy, sexually charged rendition became legendary. The dress sold for $4.8 million at auction.
rounds.fun_fact: She performed while running a 102-degree fever from a sinus infection.

rounds.question_text: One of them appeared in not one but two James Bond films — the first official one and a later parody. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ursula Andress starred as Honey Ryder in Dr. No (1962) and later as Vesper Lynd in the 1967 Bond parody Casino Royale. She remains the definitive original Bond girl.
rounds.fun_fact: Her voice in Dr. No was actually dubbed by German actress Nikki van der Zyl because the producers felt her Swiss-German accent was too strong.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred alongside Tony Curtis and Jack Lemmon in a 1959 film widely considered the greatest comedy ever made. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Marilyn Monroe starred in Some Like It Hot (1959), directed by Billy Wilder. The American Film Institute named it the funniest American film of all time.
rounds.fun_fact: Director Billy Wilder said working with Monroe was "like pulling teeth" but admitted she was "pure magic on screen."

rounds.question_text: One of them was born in Ostermundigen, Switzerland, and moved to Rome at 17 to pursue acting — appearing in Italian films before breaking into Hollywood. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ursula Andress was born in Switzerland in 1936 and moved to Italy as a teenager. She appeared in several Italian films before being cast in Dr. No, which launched her international career.
rounds.fun_fact: She dated James Dean briefly in the 1950s before his death.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at age 36 under circumstances that remain debated to this day — officially ruled a "probable suicide" from barbiturate overdose. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Marilyn Monroe was found dead on August 4, 1962, at her Los Angeles home. She was 36. The official cause was acute barbiturate poisoning, ruled a probable suicide, though conspiracy theories persist.
rounds.fun_fact: She died just weeks after the "Happy Birthday, Mr. President" performance. She was 36. Andress is still alive at 90.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups had nine core members — the largest crew in hip-hop history to function as a single unit. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wu-Tang Clan had nine original members: RZA, GZA, Ol' Dirty Bastard, Method Man, Raekwon, Ghostface Killah, Inspectah Deck, U-God, and Masta Killa.
rounds.fun_fact: Their contract structure was revolutionary — members could sign solo deals with any label, while the group stayed together on Loud Records.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups had a groundbreaking endorsement deal with Adidas — the first hip-hop sneaker deal in history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Run-D.M.C. signed a $1.6 million deal with Adidas in 1986 after their song "My Adidas" became a hit. It was the first endorsement deal between a hip-hop group and a major athletic brand.
rounds.fun_fact: At a Madison Square Garden concert, they asked fans to hold up their Adidas. An Adidas exec in the audience saw thousands of sneakers in the air and signed the deal the next day.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups collaborated with Aerosmith on a rock-rap crossover that broke down genre barriers and revived a rock band's career. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Run-D.M.C.'s 1986 collaboration with Aerosmith on "Walk This Way" became one of the most important crossover hits in music history. It revived Aerosmith's career and introduced rap to rock audiences.
rounds.fun_fact: The original Aerosmith version was from 1975. Run-D.M.C. had been sampling the beat without knowing who wrote it.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups released an album that Martin Shkreli bought the sole copy of for $2 million — making it the most expensive album ever sold. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wu-Tang Clan's Once Upon a Time in Shaolin (2015) was produced as a single copy and sold to Martin Shkreli for $2 million. The US government later seized it as part of his fraud case.
rounds.fun_fact: The album was kept in a vault in Morocco. The buyer was contractually forbidden from releasing it commercially for 88 years.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups was the first hip-hop act inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Run-D.M.C. was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2009, becoming the second hip-hop act ever inducted (after Grandmaster Flash). They were the first hip-hop group.
rounds.fun_fact: Eminem presented their induction, calling them "the Beatles of hip-hop."

rounds.question_text: One of these groups built their entire aesthetic around kung fu films, chess, and the teachings of the Five Percent Nation. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wu-Tang Clan's mythology drew heavily from martial arts cinema (particularly Shaw Brothers kung fu films), chess strategy, and Five Percent Nation philosophy. Their debut album is named after the 36th Chamber of Shaolin.
rounds.fun_fact: RZA scored several martial arts films later in his career, including The Man with the Iron Fists (2012).

rounds.question_text: One of these groups lost a founding member to a fatal shooting outside a recording studio in 2002. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Jam Master Jay (Jason Mizell) of Run-D.M.C. was shot and killed in his recording studio in Queens, New York, on October 30, 2002. He was 37. The case went unsolved for nearly 20 years before two suspects were convicted in 2024.
rounds.fun_fact: Run-D.M.C. never performed again after his death. The group was permanently retired.

rounds.question_text: One of them won six NBA championships — going 6-for-6 in Finals appearances, never losing. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michael Jordan won six titles with the Chicago Bulls (1991, 1992, 1993, 1996, 1997, 1998). He was named Finals MVP all six times. He never lost in the Finals.
rounds.fun_fact: He retired twice during his career — once to play baseball, once because he ran out of challenges.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the first-ever Slam Dunk Contest — held in the ABA, not the NBA — and popularized dunking from the free-throw line. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Julius Erving won the inaugural ABA Slam Dunk Contest at the 1976 All-Star Game in Denver, famously dunking from the free-throw line. He brought the art of dunking to mainstream basketball.
rounds.fun_fact: Jordan later perfected the free-throw line dunk in the 1988 NBA Dunk Contest — but Dr. J did it first, 12 years earlier.

rounds.question_text: One of them played through severe food poisoning in Game 5 of the 1997 NBA Finals — scoring 38 points before collapsing into a teammate's arms. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michael Jordan's "Flu Game" against the Utah Jazz is one of the most legendary performances in sports history. He scored 38 points in 44 minutes, including the go-ahead three-pointer with 25 seconds left.
rounds.fun_fact: It was later revealed to be food poisoning, not flu. His trainer Tim Grover blamed a late-night pizza delivery.

rounds.question_text: One of them is the only player in basketball history to be named MVP in both the ABA and the NBA. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Julius Erving won three ABA MVPs (1974, 1975, 1976) and one NBA MVP (1981). No other player has won the top individual award in both leagues.
rounds.fun_fact: When the ABA merged with the NBA in 1976, Dr. J was the prize acquisition. He made the merger worth it.

rounds.question_text: One of them has a shoe brand that generates over $5 billion in annual revenue — more than most NBA teams are worth. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Air Jordan brand, a subsidiary of Nike, generates over $5 billion annually. Jordan earns approximately $150 million per year from the brand alone — more than he ever earned playing basketball.
rounds.fun_fact: Nike initially offered him $500,000 per year. His mother told him to take the meeting. The brand is now worth more than many Fortune 500 companies.

rounds.question_text: One of them helped legitimize an entire rival basketball league — making the merger with the NBA possible through his sheer star power. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dr. J's dominance in the ABA gave the league credibility and forced the NBA to take it seriously. The ABA-NBA merger in 1976 happened largely because teams wanted access to players like Erving.
rounds.fun_fact: He played for the New York Nets in the ABA before being sold to the Philadelphia 76ers for $3 million after the merger.

rounds.question_text: One of them wore #23 — a number that became so iconic it was retired by a team he never even played for. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michael Jordan's #23 was retired by the Chicago Bulls and the Miami Heat (where he never played). The Heat retired it as a tribute to his impact on the entire sport.
rounds.fun_fact: He briefly wore #45 when he came back from his first retirement in 1995, then switched back to #23 because it felt wrong.

rounds.question_text: One of them played a feedback-drenched version of "The Star-Spangled Banner" at Woodstock in 1969 that became the defining musical statement of the counterculture era. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Jimi Hendrix's performance of the national anthem at Woodstock on August 18, 1969, used distortion and feedback to evoke the sounds of war. It remains the most famous guitar performance in history.
rounds.fun_fact: He took the stage at 8 AM Monday morning — rain had delayed the schedule. Only 30,000-40,000 of the original 400,000 attendees were still there.

rounds.question_text: One of them survived an assassination attempt when seven gunmen raided his home two days before a peace concert. He was shot in the arm but still performed. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Marley was shot at his home at 56 Hope Road in Kingston on December 3, 1976, amid political tensions before Jamaican elections. Despite being wounded, he performed at the Smile Jamaica Concert two days later.
rounds.fun_fact: His wife Rita was also shot — the bullet grazed her head. Both survived.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at age 27, becoming a founding member of the infamous "27 Club" alongside Janis Joplin, Jim Morrison, and later Kurt Cobain. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Jimi Hendrix died on September 18, 1970, in London at age 27 from asphyxia after ingesting barbiturates. His death, alongside Brian Jones, Janis Joplin, and Jim Morrison, established the tragic "27 Club" pattern.
rounds.fun_fact: He had been performing for only four years on the international stage. His entire recorded legacy spans 1966-1970.

rounds.question_text: One of them became the voice of an entire political and spiritual movement — with his music inseparable from Rastafari, pan-Africanism, and the struggle for liberation. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Marley's music became the global anthem of Rastafari and the pan-African liberation movement. Songs like "One Love," "Redemption Song," and "Get Up, Stand Up" transcended entertainment to become protest hymns.
rounds.fun_fact: His album Exodus (1977) was named the greatest album of the 20th century by Time magazine in 1999.

rounds.question_text: One of them was left-handed but played a right-handed Fender Stratocaster flipped upside down — and could play with his teeth, behind his back, or while setting the guitar on fire. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Hendrix played a right-handed Stratocaster restrung for left-handed playing. His showmanship included playing with his teeth, behind his head, and famously setting his guitar on fire at the Monterey Pop Festival in 1967.
rounds.fun_fact: He was self-taught. He never learned to read music.

rounds.question_text: One of them is credited with bringing an entire music genre — previously confined to a small Caribbean island — to a worldwide audience. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Marley brought reggae from Jamaica to the world stage. Before him, reggae was virtually unknown outside the Caribbean. His international tours and albums made it a global genre.
rounds.fun_fact: He died on May 11, 1981, at age 36 from melanoma that had spread from under his toenail. He refused amputation on religious grounds.

rounds.question_text: Rolling Stone magazine ranked one of them the greatest guitarist of all time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Jimi Hendrix has been ranked #1 on Rolling Stone's list of the greatest guitarists of all time in every edition of the list. No other guitarist has ever claimed the top spot.
rounds.fun_fact: He achieved this status with a career that lasted barely four years. Most guitarists on the list had decades.

rounds.question_text: One of them coined "Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee" — perhaps the most famous line in sports history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Muhammad Ali first used the phrase before his 1964 fight against Sonny Liston. The full quote: "Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee. The hands can't hit what the eyes can't see."
rounds.fun_fact: The line was actually written by his cornerman Drew Bundini Brown, but Ali made it immortal.

rounds.question_text: One of them scored 100 points in a single game — a record that has stood for over 60 years and may never be broken. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points for the Philadelphia Warriors against the New York Knicks on March 2, 1962, in Hershey, Pennsylvania. The final score was 169-147.
rounds.fun_fact: No video of the full game exists. Only an audio recording and the famous photo of Wilt holding a paper reading "100" survive.

rounds.question_text: One of them refused to be drafted into the Vietnam War, was stripped of his heavyweight title, and banned from boxing for three years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ali refused induction into the US Army in 1967, saying "I ain't got no quarrel with them Viet Cong." He was stripped of his title, convicted of draft evasion, and didn't fight for over three years during his prime.
rounds.fun_fact: The Supreme Court unanimously overturned his conviction in 1971. He came back and won the title twice more.

rounds.question_text: One of them claimed in his autobiography to have slept with 20,000 women — a number he later said was just "a nice round figure." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain made the claim in his 1991 autobiography A View From Above. He later clarified he was looking for a number that conveyed how big a role women played in his life as a bachelor athlete.
rounds.fun_fact: His teammate Jerry West said: "Wilt had a tendency to elaborate things and make him bigger than life."

rounds.question_text: These two almost fought each other in a boxing match at the Houston Astrodome in 1971. Contracts were drawn up but one of them backed out. Who was supposed to be the challenger?
rounds.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain agreed to a non-title boxing match against Ali. A press conference was planned, but Chamberlain's lawyers and his father convinced him to drop out. Ali taunted: "Timber! The tree will fall."
rounds.fun_fact: Chamberlain was 7'1" and 275 pounds. Ali was 6'3" and 215 pounds. It would have been the most insane sporting event of the decade.

rounds.question_text: One of them changed his name from Cassius Marcellus Clay Jr. after joining the Nation of Islam, and refused to answer to his old name. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ali converted to Islam and changed his name in 1964. He famously told Ernie Terrell in the ring: "What's my name?!" while punishing him for using "Clay."
rounds.fun_fact: His father, Cassius Sr., was named after the 19th-century Kentucky abolitionist Cassius Marcellus Clay.

rounds.question_text: One of them averaged 50.4 points per game for an entire NBA season — a record so absurd it sounds made up. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain averaged 50.4 points per game during the 1961-62 season. He also averaged 48.5 minutes per game that season — in a sport with 48-minute games — because of overtime.
rounds.fun_fact: He never fouled out of a single game in his entire 14-year career. Not once.

rounds.question_text: One of them said: "I spent a lot of money on booze, birds, and fast cars. The rest I just squandered." Which one?
rounds.explanation: George Best delivered this line, which became one of the most quoted sentences in football history. It perfectly captured his legendary talent and equally legendary self-destruction.
rounds.fun_fact: He also said: "In 1969 I gave up women and alcohol — it was the worst 20 minutes of my life."

rounds.question_text: One of them cried on camera during a World Cup semi-final after receiving a yellow card that would have ruled him out of the final. The image became one of football's most iconic moments. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Paul Gascoigne's tears during England's 1990 World Cup semi-final against West Germany in Turin became the defining image of Italia '90. The booking meant he'd miss the final if England won.
rounds.fun_fact: England lost on penalties. The tears were for nothing — except making him the most loved footballer in England.

rounds.question_text: One of them played for Manchester United from age 17 and scored 179 goals in 470 appearances — becoming the greatest talent Old Trafford had ever seen. Which one?
rounds.explanation: George Best joined Manchester United as a 15-year-old in 1961 and made his debut at 17. He won two league titles and the European Cup in 1968, the same year he won the Ballon d'Or.
rounds.fun_fact: Matt Busby's scout sent him a telegram after watching Best trial: "I think I've found you a genius."

rounds.question_text: One of them was photographed having alcohol poured down his throat in a dentist's chair during a team trip to Hong Kong before Euro 96. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Gascoigne was at the center of the infamous "Dentist's Chair" incident in Hong Kong in 1996. After photos leaked of players in a nightclub bar, he celebrated a goal at Euro 96 by lying on the pitch while teammates squirted water into his mouth.
rounds.fun_fact: The goal celebration re-enactment against Scotland at Wembley is one of the greatest moments in England football history.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the Ballon d'Or — European Footballer of the Year — at age 22. Which one?
rounds.explanation: George Best won the 1968 Ballon d'Or after leading Manchester United to the European Cup. He scored in the final against Benfica at Wembley.
rounds.fun_fact: Pelé said Best was "the greatest player in the world" — the highest compliment from the king himself.

rounds.question_text: One of them scored a solo goal against Scotland where he flicked the ball over Colin Hendry's head, ran around him, and volleyed it in — a goal so audacious it defined an entire tournament. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Gascoigne's goal against Scotland at Euro 96 is considered one of the greatest ever scored at a European Championship. He flicked the ball over Hendry's head with his left foot and volleyed with his right.
rounds.fun_fact: He then slid across the Wembley turf for the Dentist's Chair celebration. The whole country lost its mind.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at 59 from organ failure related to decades of alcoholism, after receiving a liver transplant three years earlier. Which one?
rounds.explanation: George Best died on November 25, 2005, at age 59. His body failed after years of alcohol abuse, despite a liver transplant in 2002. He once said from his hospital bed: "Don't die like me."
rounds.fun_fact: Gascoigne, now in his late 50s, continues to battle addiction publicly. Both men's stories are warnings wrapped in genius.

rounds.question_text: One of them invented a save so outrageous it got its own name — diving forward and kicking the ball away with both heels behind his back at Wembley in 1995. Which one?
rounds.explanation: René Higuita performed the "Scorpion Kick" save against England at Wembley on September 6, 1995, clearing a Jamie Redknapp cross. It became one of the most replayed moments in football history.
rounds.fun_fact: He got the idea after watching a child do a similar kick during a TV commercial shoot.

rounds.question_text: One of them suffered a seizure hours before a World Cup final, was initially dropped from the team sheet, then mysteriously reinstated and played poorly as his team lost 3-0. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ronaldo had convulsions in his hotel room before the 1998 World Cup final against France. He was unconscious for 30-40 seconds. He insisted on playing despite being visibly unwell. Brazil lost 3-0.
rounds.fun_fact: Conspiracy theories persist. Some blamed Nike's sponsorship pressure. Ronaldo himself has never fully explained what happened.

rounds.question_text: One of them was jailed for seven months for acting as a go-between in a kidnapping linked to Pablo Escobar. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Higuita was imprisoned in 1993 for delivering ransom money that secured the release of a drug cartel associate's daughter. He went on a two-week hunger strike in prison before being released without charge.
rounds.fun_fact: He has never hidden his friendship with Escobar and visited him in prison.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the World Cup in 2002 despite being told his career was over after multiple devastating knee injuries. He scored both goals in the final. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ronaldo suffered two career-threatening knee injuries (1999 and 2000) but came back to win the 2002 World Cup, scoring 8 goals in the tournament including both in the 2-0 final against Germany.
rounds.fun_fact: His iconic shaved-head-with-a-front-triangle hairstyle from that tournament was deliberately ugly. He said he did it so reporters would ask about his hair instead of his knee.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a goalkeeper who regularly dribbled the ball past the halfway line, took free kicks, and played like an outfield player — earning the nickname "El Loco." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Higuita was famous for abandoning his goal to dribble upfield. His style revolutionized the goalkeeper position but also led to disasters — most famously when Roger Milla tackled him in the 1990 World Cup, ending Colombia's run.
rounds.fun_fact: He is considered a spiritual predecessor to Manuel Neuer's "sweeper-keeper" style.

rounds.question_text: One of them scored 352 goals in 518 career matches, including 62 in 98 appearances for his national team, and was named FIFA World Player of the Year three times. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ronaldo won FIFA World Player of the Year in 1996, 1997, and 2002. He scored for Barcelona, Inter Milan, Real Madrid, and AC Milan across a legendary career.
rounds.fun_fact: Zinedine Zidane called him "the greatest player I ever played with or against."

rounds.question_text: One of them was as famous for his wild, curly hair as for his goalkeeping — the hair became inseparable from the legend. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Higuita's massive mane of curly hair became his visual trademark, as recognizable as his scorpion kick. It made him look even more unhinged as he charged upfield with the ball.
rounds.fun_fact: Ronaldo's 2002 triangle haircut was deliberately ridiculous. Higuita's hair was just... Higuita.

rounds.question_text: One of them became the first Black man to win the US Open (1968) and Wimbledon (1975) — breaking through racial barriers in a sport that had excluded Black players for decades. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Arthur Ashe won the US Open in 1968 and Wimbledon in 1975. He remains the only Black man to have won singles titles at Wimbledon, the US Open, and the Australian Open.
rounds.fun_fact: He grew up in segregated Richmond, Virginia, where he was not allowed to play on public tennis courts.

rounds.question_text: One of them is the last man from his country to win his home Grand Slam — a drought that has lasted over 40 years and counting. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Yannick Noah won the French Open in 1983. No Frenchman has won Roland Garros since. The drought is one of the longest in tennis.
rounds.fun_fact: He beat Mats Wilander in the final. The entire stadium erupted. It remains one of the most emotional moments in French sports history.

rounds.question_text: One of them was discovered as a child playing tennis in Cameroon by a visiting American tennis legend — who sponsored his move to Europe to train. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Yannick Noah was spotted by Arthur Ashe himself during a tennis clinic in Yaoundé, Cameroon, in 1971. Ashe helped arrange for the 11-year-old Noah to train at the French Tennis Federation.
rounds.fun_fact: The man who discovered him was the other fighter in this matchup. Their stories are forever linked.

rounds.question_text: One of them became a successful pop singer after retiring from tennis, releasing multiple albums and performing across Europe. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Yannick Noah launched a music career in 1991 with the album Black & What. His single "Saga Africa" became a massive hit in France. He has released multiple albums and performs to large audiences.
rounds.fun_fact: He returned to Roland Garros 40 years after his 1983 title — this time with a microphone, not a racket.

rounds.question_text: One of them was arrested protesting apartheid outside the South African embassy in Washington, D.C. — fighting for the same cause that had banned him from playing in the South African Open. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Arthur Ashe was a vocal critic of apartheid and was arrested in 1985 during an anti-apartheid protest outside the South African embassy. South Africa had denied him a visa to play in 1969.
