rounds.fun_fact: He co-founded Artists and Athletes Against Apartheid and campaigned until the system fell.

rounds.question_text: One of them defeated the defending champion and overwhelming favorite Jimmy Connors in the Wimbledon final — using a tactical masterclass of slow, soft shots to neutralize Connors's power. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Arthur Ashe defeated Jimmy Connors in the 1975 Wimbledon final using a strategy of junk balls, slices, and changes of pace. Nobody expected it to work. It was chess against a sledgehammer.
rounds.fun_fact: Ashe was 31. Most people thought he was past his prime. He proved them all wrong.

rounds.question_text: One of them contracted HIV from a blood transfusion during heart surgery — and spent his final years educating the public about AIDS before dying at 49. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Arthur Ashe contracted HIV from a blood transfusion during heart bypass surgery in 1983. He went public in 1992 and spent his remaining time as an AIDS awareness advocate. He died on February 6, 1993.
rounds.fun_fact: The main stadium at the US Open is named Arthur Ashe Stadium. It is the largest tennis arena in the world.

rounds.question_text: One of them broke the world record by nearly two feet in a single jump — a margin so absurd that he collapsed in shock and had to be helped off the field. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Beamon leapt 8.90 meters (29 feet 2¼ inches) at the 1968 Mexico City Olympics, breaking the existing record by 55 cm. Officials had to use a tape measure because the optical device didn't reach far enough.
rounds.fun_fact: When told the distance, Beamon collapsed. His teammate Ralph Boston said: "You have destroyed this event."

rounds.question_text: One of them invented a technique so revolutionary that within 4 years, the majority of competitors had adopted it — and today virtually every athlete in the sport uses it. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dick Fosbury introduced the "Fosbury Flop" — going over the bar back-first instead of face-down. At the 1972 Olympics, 28 of 40 high jumpers used his technique. Today, every competitive high jumper does.
rounds.fun_fact: Before Fosbury, jumpers went over face-down (straddle technique). His coach initially tried to talk him out of the back-first approach.

rounds.question_text: Both competed at the same Olympics. One won gold in the long jump, the other in the high jump. Which city hosted those Games?
rounds.explanation: Both Bob Beamon and Dick Fosbury won gold medals at the 1968 Summer Olympics in Mexico City. The thin air at altitude (2,240 meters) contributed to Beamon's extraordinary distance.
rounds.fun_fact: The 1968 Mexico City Olympics were also where Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised their fists on the podium.

rounds.question_text: One of them set a world record that stood for 23 years — from 1968 until 1991. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Beamon's long jump record of 8.90m stood from October 18, 1968, until August 30, 1991, when Mike Powell jumped 8.95m in Tokyo. It remains the Olympic record to this day.
rounds.fun_fact: For context, the previous record had been broken 13 times in the 8 years before Beamon. His record lasted nearly a quarter century.

rounds.question_text: One of them set a new Olympic record of 2.24 meters (7 feet 4¼ inches) using a technique that spectators initially laughed at because it looked so strange. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dick Fosbury won gold at 2.24m with his back-first technique. Crowds and commentators were baffled by his approach, but the results were undeniable.
rounds.fun_fact: His high school coach had begged him to use the conventional straddle technique. Fosbury ignored him and changed the sport forever.

rounds.question_text: One of them had his technique named after him — a term that is now in the dictionary and used in business and innovation metaphors worldwide. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The "Fosbury Flop" is one of the most famous named techniques in all of sports. The term has entered common language as a metaphor for unconventional thinking that revolutionizes an entire field.
rounds.fun_fact: A newspaper reporter in Fosbury's hometown of Medford, Oregon, coined the term. Fosbury himself just called it "going over backwards."

rounds.question_text: One of them never came close to repeating his Olympic performance — his greatest moment was so far beyond anything else he ever did that it seemed like a miracle. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bob Beamon never jumped beyond 27 feet again after his 29-foot-2 leap in Mexico City. He retired from athletics in 1976 without ever approaching the record. It truly was a once-in-a-lifetime moment.
rounds.fun_fact: Scientists later calculated that everything aligned: altitude, wind, takeoff angle, and speed. It was a perfect storm of physics and human ability.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups recorded the soundtrack to a 1977 film that sold over 40 million copies — making it one of the best-selling albums of all time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Bee Gees wrote and performed the Saturday Night Fever soundtrack, which sold over 40 million copies worldwide. It defined the disco era and turned them into global superstars.
rounds.fun_fact: The soundtrack contains five #1 singles. It spent 24 consecutive weeks at #1 on the Billboard 200.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups was the first Western pop act to perform in communist China — their manager spent 18 months bribing officials to make it happen. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wham! performed in Beijing and Guangzhou in April 1985, becoming the first Western pop group to play in the People's Republic of China. Manager Simon Napier-Bell spent months negotiating with Chinese officials.
rounds.fun_fact: The Chinese audience sat in confused silence for much of the show. They had never seen anything like it.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups released a Christmas single that took 36 years to reach #1 in the UK — finally topping the chart in 2021. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Wham!'s "Last Christmas" was released in 1984 but was kept off #1 by Band Aid's "Do They Know It's Christmas." It finally reached #1 on New Year's Day 2021 and became Christmas #1 in 2023.
rounds.fun_fact: It is now the third best-selling UK single of all time with 5.34 million combined sales.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups recorded a song whose tempo (103 BPM) is now used worldwide to train people how to perform CPR at the correct chest compression rate. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Bee Gees' "Stayin' Alive" has a tempo of approximately 103 beats per minute — matching the ideal rate of 100-120 chest compressions per minute recommended for CPR.
rounds.fun_fact: Medical studies confirmed that people who hum "Stayin' Alive" while performing CPR maintain better compression rates. The song is literally saving lives.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups secretly wrote hit songs for other artists — including Barbra Streisand's "Woman in Love" and Dionne Warwick's "Heartbreaker." Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Bee Gees (primarily Barry Gibb) wrote songs for numerous other artists. Barry Gibb wrote Streisand's "Woman in Love" (#1 worldwide) and co-produced her Guilty album, which sold 20 million copies.
rounds.fun_fact: They also wrote "Islands in the Stream" for Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton, and "Chain Reaction" for Diana Ross.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups was written by a member who got the idea from a misspelled note on a bedroom door: "Wake me up up before you go go." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Andrew Ridgeley left a note on his bedroom door for his parents that accidentally read "Wake me up up before you go go." George Michael saw it, laughed, and turned it into one of the biggest pop hits of the 1980s.
rounds.fun_fact: The song hit #1 in both the US and UK. The music video's neon colors and "Choose Life" t-shirts defined 1984.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups lost three of its members — leaving only one surviving brother from the original lineup. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Three of the four Gibb brothers have died: Andy (1988, age 30), Maurice (2003, age 53), and Robin (2012, age 62). Barry Gibb, now in his late 70s, is the sole surviving brother.
rounds.fun_fact: Wham! lost George Michael on Christmas Day 2016 at age 53. Andrew Ridgeley is still alive.

rounds.question_text: Both studied method acting under Lee Strasberg at the same legendary institution. One of them stayed awake for three days straight to play a sleep-deprived character in Marathon Man. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dustin Hoffman famously stayed awake for days to capture the exhaustion of his character in Marathon Man (1976). His co-star Laurence Olivier reportedly said: "My dear boy, why don't you just try acting?"
rounds.fun_fact: Both Pacino and Hoffman studied at the Actors Studio under Strasberg. They represent two sides of the same method.

rounds.question_text: One of them played Michael Corleone — a role that defined the modern gangster film and earned him his first Oscar nomination at age 32. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Al Pacino played Michael Corleone in The Godfather (1972) and The Godfather Part II (1974). He was nominated for Best Supporting Actor for the first film and Best Actor for the sequel.
rounds.fun_fact: Every major studio wanted Robert Redford for the role. Director Francis Ford Coppola fought to cast the unknown Pacino.

rounds.question_text: One of them played a man who disguises himself as a woman to land an acting role — a comedy that earned 10 Oscar nominations. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dustin Hoffman starred as Michael Dorsey / Dorothy Michaels in Tootsie (1982). The film received 10 Academy Award nominations and is considered one of the greatest comedies ever made.
rounds.fun_fact: Hoffman later said the role changed how he understood women: "I think I'm an interesting woman... and I know that if I met myself at a party, I would never talk to that character."

rounds.question_text: One of them was nominated for the Oscar eight times before finally winning — the longest losing streak for an actor who eventually won. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Al Pacino was nominated eight times (starting with The Godfather in 1973) before winning Best Actor for Scent of a Woman in 1993 — a 20-year wait.
rounds.fun_fact: Many consider Scent of a Woman one of his weaker performances. The Academy was making up for lost time.

rounds.question_text: One of them won two Best Actor Oscars — for Kramer vs. Kramer and Rain Man. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dustin Hoffman won Best Actor for Kramer vs. Kramer (1979) and Rain Man (1988). His portrayal of autistic savant Raymond Babbitt in Rain Man became one of the most studied performances in acting history.
rounds.fun_fact: He has seven total Oscar nominations. Pacino has nine.

rounds.question_text: One of them delivered the line "Say hello to my little friend" before unleashing a grenade launcher in a film that flopped at release but became a cultural monument. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Al Pacino as Tony Montana in Scarface (1983). The film was a box office disappointment and critically savaged, but became one of the most quoted and referenced films in hip-hop and pop culture.
rounds.fun_fact: Pacino said Scarface is the film he most wished he'd been Oscar-nominated for.

rounds.question_text: One of them was 30 years old and unknown when he was cast as a 21-year-old college graduate seduced by an older woman — launching his career overnight. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Dustin Hoffman was 30 when he starred as Benjamin Braddock in The Graduate (1967). He was cast despite being nearly a decade older than the character. The film made him an instant star.
rounds.fun_fact: He earned $17,000 for The Graduate. The film grossed $104 million.

rounds.question_text: One of them made the white disco suit iconic — a single outfit that defined an entire decade's nightlife. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Travolta's white three-piece suit in Saturday Night Fever (1977) became the most recognizable outfit in disco history. The suit sold at auction for $145,000 in 1995.
rounds.fun_fact: He was only 23 when he filmed it. The dance sequences were largely improvised.

rounds.question_text: One of them delivered "Go ahead, make my day" — a line so famous that President Reagan quoted it in a speech to Congress. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Clint Eastwood as Dirty Harry in Sudden Impact (1983). Reagan used the line in a 1985 tax policy speech, cementing it in American political culture.
rounds.fun_fact: The line was written by screenwriter Charles B. Pierce, not Eastwood. But nobody remembers that.

rounds.question_text: One of them had the greatest career comeback in Hollywood history when Quentin Tarantino cast him in a 1994 film after years in straight-to-video obscurity. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Travolta's career had flatlined before Quentin Tarantino cast him as Vincent Vega in Pulp Fiction (1994). The role earned him his second Oscar nomination and relaunched his career overnight.
rounds.fun_fact: Tarantino wrote the part specifically for Travolta. Nobody else was ever considered.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the Academy Award for Best Director twice — for a revisionist Western and a boxing drama. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Clint Eastwood won Best Director for Unforgiven (1992) and Million Dollar Baby (2004). Unforgiven also won Best Picture, making him one of the few actor-directors to achieve the double.
rounds.fun_fact: He has directed over 40 films. He shoots fast, rarely does more than two takes, and finishes under budget.

rounds.question_text: One of them has been one of the most prominent members of Scientology since 1975, crediting the church with saving his life. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Travolta joined Scientology in 1975 and has been one of the church's most visible celebrity members for nearly 50 years. He starred in the Scientology-backed film Battlefield Earth (2000), widely considered one of the worst films ever made.
rounds.fun_fact: He once said Scientology "has better tools than Catholicism."

rounds.question_text: One of them directed a critically acclaimed film at age 84, continuing to work into his 90s — one of the longest active careers in Hollywood history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Clint Eastwood directed Cry Macho at age 91 and Juror #2 at age 94. His career spans from the 1950s TV show Rawhide to the 2020s — over seven decades of continuous work.
rounds.fun_fact: He was already 62 when he won his first Oscar. Most actors retire by that age.

rounds.question_text: One of them ordered a "Royale with Cheese" in a scene that became one of the most quoted dialogues in film history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Travolta as Vincent Vega discusses the metric system and European fast food with Samuel L. Jackson in Pulp Fiction. The "Royale with Cheese" exchange is endlessly quoted.
rounds.fun_fact: Tarantino wrote the scene based on a real conversation. In Paris, McDonald's really does call it a Royal Cheese.

rounds.question_text: One of them escaped an abusive marriage that included broken bones, burned skin, and rapes — then rebuilt her career from scratch and became bigger than ever. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tina Turner left Ike Turner in 1976 after years of documented physical and sexual abuse. Her 1986 autobiography I, Tina detailed the horrors. She rebuilt from nightclub acts to arena tours.
rounds.fun_fact: She left with 36 cents and a Mobil credit card. Within a decade she was selling out stadiums worldwide.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the Academy Award for Best Actress — proving she was more than a singer. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Cher won the Oscar for Best Actress for Moonstruck in 1988. She had previously been dismissed by Hollywood as "just a pop star" and showed up to the ceremony in a famously outrageous outfit.
rounds.fun_fact: When she won, she said: "I don't think this means I'm somebody... but I guess I'm on my way."

rounds.question_text: One of them released a 1998 hit that pioneered the use of Auto-Tune as a creative vocal effect — changing pop music production forever. The technique was named after her. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Cher's "Believe" (1998) was the first hit to use Auto-Tune as a deliberate vocal distortion effect. Producers initially tried to keep the technique secret. It became known as the "Cher Effect."
rounds.fun_fact: She was 52 when the song hit #1 worldwide. It revitalized a career that was already four decades long.

rounds.question_text: One of them released Private Dancer in 1984 — a comeback album that sold over 20 million copies and proved the greatest second act in music history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tina Turner's Private Dancer (1984) went 5x platinum in the US alone. It featured "What's Love Got to Do with It" — her first and only solo #1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
rounds.fun_fact: She was 44 years old. The music industry had written her off. She outsold every female artist that year.

rounds.question_text: One of them was part of a married performing duo in the 1960s-70s that had their own TV variety show. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Cher performed with husband Sonny Bono as Sonny & Cher. Their TV show The Sonny & Cher Comedy Hour ran from 1971-1974 on CBS. They divorced in 1975.
rounds.fun_fact: Sonny Bono later became mayor of Palm Springs and a US Congressman. He died in a skiing accident in 1998.

rounds.question_text: One of them holds the record for the longest-running farewell tour in music history — a "farewell" that lasted three years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Cher's Living Proof: The Farewell Tour ran from 2002 to 2005, grossing over $250 million. She then came out of "retirement" and launched a Las Vegas residency. She's still performing.
rounds.fun_fact: Tina Turner's actual farewell was more final. Her Tina!: 50th Anniversary Tour (2008-2009) was her last. She died in May 2023.

rounds.question_text: One of them was as famous for her legs as for her voice — performing in miniature skirts and heels well into her 70s, with the most famous legs in rock and roll. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tina Turner's legs became an inseparable part of her brand. She performed in high heels and short skirts throughout her career, and her legs were reportedly insured for $3.2 million.
rounds.fun_fact: She once said: "My legacy is that I stayed on course from the beginning to the end, because I believed in something inside of me."

rounds.question_text: One of them joined the Saturday Night Live cast at just 19 years old — and single-handedly saved the show from cancellation with his performances. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy joined SNL in 1980 at age 19. The show was struggling after the original cast left, and Murphy's characters (Gumby, Buckwheat, Mr. Robinson) revived it.
rounds.fun_fact: He was initially hired as an extra/featured player. Within weeks he was the breakout star.

rounds.question_text: One of them co-created a fictional blues band for SNL that became so popular it spawned a #1 album and a hit movie. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Belushi and Dan Aykroyd created The Blues Brothers for SNL. The 1980 film became a cult classic, and the Briefcase Full of Blues album reached #1 on the Billboard 200 in 1978.
rounds.fun_fact: The Blues Brothers film holds the record for most cars destroyed in a movie — over 100.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in Beverly Hills Cop (1984) — the highest-grossing film of that year — and 48 Hrs., Trading Places, and Coming to America in the span of six years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy dominated 1980s box offices. Beverly Hills Cop grossed $316 million worldwide. Between 1982 and 1988, he starred in five consecutive box office hits.
rounds.fun_fact: He was offered the role of Winston in Ghostbusters (1984) but turned it down. The role went to Errol Hudson.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in Animal House (1978) — a college comedy so successful that toga parties became a nationwide phenomenon. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Belushi played Bluto Blutarsky in National Lampoon's Animal House. The film grossed $141 million on a $2.8 million budget and invented the modern gross-out comedy genre.
rounds.fun_fact: Belushi's food fight scene and "Was it over when the Germans bombed Pearl Harbor?" speech were largely improvised.

rounds.question_text: One of them voiced Donkey in the Shrek franchise and Mushu in Mulan — becoming one of the most successful voice actors in animation history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy voiced Donkey in all four Shrek films (2001-2010) and the dragon Mushu in Mulan (1998). The Shrek franchise grossed over $3.5 billion worldwide.
rounds.fun_fact: He was the first actor to have two films open at #1 in the same weekend: Shrek 2 and Daddy Day Care were both in theaters simultaneously.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at the Chateau Marmont hotel on the Sunset Strip at age 33 — from a lethal injection of heroin and cocaine administered by a companion. Which one?
rounds.explanation: John Belushi died on March 5, 1982, at the Chateau Marmont in Los Angeles. His companion Cathy Smith later pleaded guilty to administering the fatal "speedball" injection.
rounds.fun_fact: He had been partying with Robin Williams and Robert De Niro earlier that evening. Both left before the fatal dose.

rounds.question_text: One of them made a triumphant return to SNL in 2019 after 35 years away — hosting for the first time since leaving the show and winning an Emmy for the episode. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy hosted SNL on December 21, 2019, his first time back since 1984. He revived his classic characters and won the Primetime Emmy for Outstanding Guest Actor in a Comedy Series.
rounds.fun_fact: Belushi never got a second act. Murphy got dozens. That's the difference 33 years of life makes.

tagline: The King of Cool vs The Blue Eyes

rounds.question_text: When these two starred in The Towering Inferno (1974), one of them demanded equal billing, equal lines, AND the last line of the movie. Their names were displayed diagonally on the poster so neither appeared "above" the other. Who made the demands?
rounds.explanation: Steve McQueen was obsessively competitive with Newman. He counted lines in the script and demanded the final line in the film regardless of whether it made narrative sense. Newman called him "chicken s--t."
rounds.fun_fact: The diagonal billing was a Hollywood first — McQueen's name was positioned lower-left, Newman upper-right, so both could claim top billing.

rounds.question_text: One of them founded a food company whose products have generated over $600 million for children's charities — starting with a homemade salad dressing. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Paul Newman founded Newman's Own in 1982 with author A.E. Hotchner, starting with salad dressing they'd been giving to friends. The company donates 100% of after-tax profits to charity.
rounds.fun_fact: They launched with $40,000 combined. Newman said: "The embarrassing thing is that the salad dressing is outgrossing my films."

rounds.question_text: One of them was so obsessed with racing that he competed in the 12 Hours of Sebring, nearly entered Le Mans, and made an entire movie about the race as a passion project. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Steve McQueen was a serious racing driver who competed professionally. His 1971 film Le Mans was a passion project that nearly bankrupted the studio. He said: "Racing is life. Anything before or after is just waiting."
rounds.fun_fact: He often raced under the fake name "Harvey Mushman" to avoid attention from studios who banned him from racing.

rounds.question_text: One of them also raced cars professionally — finishing second at the 24 Hours of Le Mans at age 70. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Paul Newman was also a serious racing driver. He finished second in class at the 1979 24 Hours of Le Mans and continued racing into his 80s. He won four national championships with the Sports Car Club of America.
rounds.fun_fact: Both were elite racing drivers. This is the only Hollywood rivalry where both men could actually beat you on a racetrack.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in The Great Escape (1963), doing many of his own motorcycle stunts in one of cinema's most famous chase sequences. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Steve McQueen played Captain Hilts in The Great Escape. The iconic motorcycle jump attempt at the Swiss border fence became one of the most famous stunts in film history.
rounds.fun_fact: McQueen actually did most of the riding himself, but the final fence jump was performed by his friend Bud Ekins.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in a 1967 film as a defiant prisoner who refuses to break, delivering the line: "What we've got here is failure to communicate." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Paul Newman starred in Cool Hand Luke (1967), playing a rebellious prisoner in a Florida chain gang. The film's famous line was delivered by the Captain (Strother Martin), not Newman's character.
rounds.fun_fact: Newman was nominated for the Oscar but lost. The egg-eating contest scene — 50 hard-boiled eggs in one hour — is one of the most iconic scenes in American cinema.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at 50, the other lived to 83. Who died young?
rounds.explanation: Steve McQueen died on November 7, 1980, at age 50, from mesothelioma (a cancer linked to asbestos exposure). Paul Newman died on September 26, 2008, at age 83, from lung cancer.
rounds.fun_fact: McQueen was the highest-paid movie star in the world at the time of his death. Newman spent his final decades racing cars and giving away hundreds of millions to charity.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a red swimsuit poster that sold 12 million copies — the best-selling poster in history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Farrah Fawcett's 1976 red swimsuit poster sold 12 million copies, making it the best-selling poster of all time. It became a cultural icon of the late 1970s.
rounds.fun_fact: She earned just $15,000 for the photo shoot. The poster outsold every poster before or since.

rounds.question_text: One of them played Catwoman in a Tim Burton film — a performance widely considered the definitive portrayal of the character. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michelle Pfeiffer played Selina Kyle / Catwoman in Batman Returns (1992). Her performance is still regarded as the greatest portrayal of Catwoman in any medium.
rounds.fun_fact: She learned to crack a bullwhip for the role and did many of her own stunts in the latex catsuit.

rounds.question_text: One of them became a global phenomenon through a TV show about three female private detectives — but quit after just one season. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Farrah Fawcett starred in Charlie's Angels (1976) but left after the first season, feeling creatively stifled. Her departure made headlines worldwide and led to a lawsuit from the production company.
rounds.fun_fact: She returned as a guest star in six episodes across seasons 3 and 4 as part of a legal settlement.

rounds.question_text: One of them played Elvira Hancock opposite Al Pacino in Scarface (1983) — a breakout role that launched her film career. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michelle Pfeiffer played Elvira, Tony Montana's icy wife, in Brian De Palma's Scarface. The role established her as a serious film actress beyond her beauty.
rounds.fun_fact: She was terrified during filming. She later said she had no idea what she was doing and was intimidated by Pacino.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a feathered hairstyle so influential that millions of women brought her photo to salons — the "feathered look" became the defining hairstyle of the late 1970s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Farrah Fawcett's feathered, blown-back hairstyle became the most copied hairstyle of the 1970s. Her hairdresser Allen Edwards created the look using layers and a blow dryer.
rounds.fun_fact: The hairstyle is still known simply as "The Farrah" — no last name needed.

rounds.question_text: One of them earned three Oscar nominations across three decades — for Dangerous Liaisons (1988), The Fabulous Baker Boys (1989), and Love Field (1992). Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michelle Pfeiffer received three Best Actress/Supporting Actress nominations in four years, proving extraordinary range from period drama to musical romance to racial drama.
rounds.fun_fact: She never won the Oscar. Many consider her the most talented actress of her generation to be shut out.

rounds.question_text: One of them died on the same day as Michael Jackson — June 25, 2009 — with her death overshadowed by the global coverage of his. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Farrah Fawcett died of anal cancer on the morning of June 25, 2009, at age 62. Hours later, Michael Jackson died of cardiac arrest, dominating the news cycle and eclipsing her death.
rounds.fun_fact: Her documentary Farrah's Story, about her cancer battle, had been watched by 9 million viewers just weeks earlier.

rounds.question_text: One of them released Back to Black (2006) — an album that sold over 20 million copies and is considered one of the greatest records of the 21st century. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Amy Winehouse's Back to Black won five Grammy Awards and revived interest in classic soul, jazz, and girl-group production. It remains one of the best-selling albums in UK history.
rounds.fun_fact: She wrote most of it about her toxic on-off relationship with Blake Fielder-Civil, whom she later married.

rounds.question_text: One of them is credited with launching the entire neo-soul genre — her 1997 debut album placed her at the forefront of a movement that redefined R&B. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Erykah Badu's Baduizm (1997) is widely considered the album that launched neo-soul. Music critics dubbed her the "Queen of Neo-Soul" and the "First Lady of Neo-Soul."
rounds.fun_fact: Her career began after opening for D'Angelo in 1994 in Fort Worth, Texas.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at age 27 from alcohol poisoning — joining the tragic "27 Club" alongside Jimi Hendrix, Jim Morrison, and Kurt Cobain. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Amy Winehouse died on July 23, 2011, in London. Her blood alcohol level was more than five times the legal drink-drive limit. She was 27.
rounds.fun_fact: Her father Mitch said she had told him three weeks earlier that she had stopped drinking. The relapse was fatal.

rounds.question_text: One of them is as famous for her towering head wraps and Afrocentric style as for her music — using fashion as a form of spiritual and cultural expression. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Erykah Badu's head wraps became her visual trademark, representing her connection to African heritage, spirituality, and self-expression. They are inseparable from her artistic identity.
rounds.fun_fact: She has said the wraps represent her "queendom" and her refusal to conform to Western beauty standards.

rounds.question_text: One of them won five Grammy Awards in a single night — the most by a British artist at that time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Amy Winehouse won five Grammys at the 2008 ceremony for Back to Black, including Record of the Year, Song of the Year, and Best New Artist. She couldn't attend in person because her US visa was denied.
rounds.fun_fact: She performed via satellite from London. She looked stunned when her name was called — genuinely shocked.

rounds.question_text: One of them is known for concerts that can last 4+ hours, blending music with spiritual ceremony, comedy, and audience communion — no two shows are ever alike. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Erykah Badu's live shows are legendary for their length and unpredictability. She improvises extensively, incorporates spirituality and humor, and often turns concerts into communal experiences.
rounds.fun_fact: She has been actively performing for over 25 years and continues to sell out venues worldwide.

rounds.question_text: One of them sang "They tried to make me go to rehab, I said no, no, no" — a lyric that became tragically prophetic. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Amy Winehouse's "Rehab" (2006) was based on her real refusal to attend rehab at the urging of her management. The song won three Grammys. She died of alcohol-related causes five years later.
rounds.fun_fact: The song was written in its entirety in just a few hours. Mark Ronson produced it to sound like a 1960s Motown record.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the #1 overall pick in the 1985 NBA Draft — a pick that was decided by the first-ever NBA Draft Lottery. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Patrick Ewing was selected first overall by the New York Knicks in 1985. The draft lottery was introduced that year specifically because teams were suspected of tanking to get a shot at Ewing.
rounds.fun_fact: The Knicks won the lottery with a 1-in-7 chance. Conspiracy theories about a frozen envelope persist to this day.

rounds.question_text: One of them died of a cocaine overdose just two days after being selected #2 overall in the 1986 NBA Draft by the Boston Celtics. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Len Bias was picked second overall on June 17, 1986, and died on June 19 from cardiac arrhythmia caused by cocaine. He was 22 years old. It is considered the greatest "what if" in NBA history.
rounds.fun_fact: His death directly led to the Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1986, which introduced mandatory minimum sentences for drug offenses and reshaped US drug policy for decades.

rounds.question_text: One of them was supposed to extend the Boston Celtics dynasty alongside Larry Bird — but never played a single NBA game. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Len Bias was drafted by the Celtics to be Larry Bird's successor. Had he played, the Celtics dynasty of the 1980s might have continued well into the 1990s. Instead, the franchise declined.
rounds.fun_fact: Bird later said losing Bias was devastating: "That was a turning point for our franchise."

rounds.question_text: One of them played 15 seasons for the New York Knicks, made 11 All-Star teams, and became the franchise's all-time leading scorer — but never won a championship. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Patrick Ewing spent 15 years with the Knicks, averaging 21 points and 9.8 rebounds. He made the NBA Finals in 1994 but lost to the Houston Rockets. He is the greatest player to never win a ring in Knicks history.
rounds.fun_fact: He scored 24,815 career points. The only thing missing from his resume is a championship.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the ACC Player of the Year at the University of Maryland and was considered the most exciting college player since Michael Jordan. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Len Bias was a two-time ACC Player of the Year at Maryland (1985, 1986). He averaged 23.2 points and 6.8 rebounds in his senior season. Scouts compared him directly to Michael Jordan.
rounds.fun_fact: Jordan himself said Bias was one of the few players who could match him physically. They were built almost identically.

rounds.question_text: One of them won Olympic gold medals in 1984 and 1992 — the second time as part of the legendary "Dream Team." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Patrick Ewing won gold at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics and again at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics as a member of the Dream Team alongside Jordan, Bird, and Magic Johnson.
rounds.fun_fact: The 1992 Dream Team is widely considered the greatest sports team ever assembled. They won every game by an average of 44 points.

rounds.question_text: One of them's death at 22 led directly to a federal law that introduced mandatory minimum sentences for drug offenses — reshaping American criminal justice for decades. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1986 was fast-tracked through Congress in the wake of Len Bias's death. It created the controversial 100-to-1 sentencing disparity between crack and powder cocaine.
rounds.fun_fact: Legal scholars have called it one of the most consequential pieces of legislation in US history — all triggered by the death of a 22-year-old basketball player who never played a professional game.

rounds.question_text: One of them won the Academy Award for Best Original Song — becoming the first African American to win an Oscar in a non-acting category. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes won the Oscar for "Theme from Shaft" at the 1972 ceremony. He performed the song shirtless and draped in gold chains on the Academy Awards stage.
rounds.fun_fact: He beat out songs from Willy Wonka and Bedknobs and Broomsticks. The coolest man at the Oscars that night — by a mile.

rounds.question_text: One of them founded a band that blended funk, soul, jazz, R&B, pop, and African rhythms into something entirely new — eventually selling over 90 million records. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maurice White founded Earth, Wind & Fire in 1969 in Chicago. The band's fusion of genres was revolutionary, and they became one of the best-selling bands in history with over 90 million records sold.
rounds.fun_fact: The band's name comes from White's astrological chart — his sign had earth, air (wind), and fire as dominant elements. There's no water.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a spoken-word voice so deep and distinctive that he became the voice of Chef on South Park for 14 seasons. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes voiced Chef on South Park from 1997 to 2006. The character was beloved for his deep baritone and his habit of breaking into seductive songs. Hayes left the show over its satirization of Scientology.
rounds.fun_fact: He was a practicing Scientologist. When South Park mocked the religion, he quit — despite years of the show mocking every other religion without complaint.

rounds.question_text: One of them wrote "September" — a song so universally joyful that it has become the most-played song at parties, weddings, and celebrations worldwide. Do you remember the 21st night of September? Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maurice White co-wrote "September" for Earth, Wind & Fire. Released in 1978, the song has become the definitive celebration anthem. It re-enters charts every September 21st.
rounds.fun_fact: The songwriter who helped White write it asked why the date was the 21st. White said: "It just sounded good. No other reason."

rounds.question_text: One of them was as famous for his gold chains and shaved head as for his music — creating a visual image that influenced hip-hop fashion decades later. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes's bald head and gold chains became his signature look. He performed at the Oscars draped in them. His visual style directly influenced the aesthetics of 1980s and 1990s hip-hop.
rounds.fun_fact: He was nicknamed "Black Moses" — partly for his deep spiritual voice, partly because he looked like a god walking among mortals.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a classically trained musician who played the kalimba (African thumb piano) as a signature element of the band's sound — bringing African instrumentation into mainstream pop. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Maurice White played the kalimba throughout Earth, Wind & Fire's career, making the African thumb piano a recognizable element of their sound. It connected the band's music to its African roots.
rounds.fun_fact: He also played drums, percussion, and vocals. He was a multi-instrumentalist who studied at the Chicago Conservatory of Music.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a key songwriter and session musician at Stax Records in Memphis — helping craft the "Memphis Soul" sound that defined 1960s R&B alongside Otis Redding and Sam & Dave. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes was a songwriter and session musician at Stax Records, co-writing hits like "Soul Man" and "Hold On, I'm Comin'" with David Porter for Sam & Dave. He helped build the Memphis soul sound.
rounds.fun_fact: He didn't become a solo star until his late 20s. Before Shaft, he was the man behind the curtain — writing hits for everyone else.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups released the first rap album ever to reach #1 on the Billboard 200 — a punk-rap debut that sold 10 million copies. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Beastie Boys' Licensed to Ill topped the Billboard 200 on March 7, 1987, becoming the first hip-hop album to reach #1. It was certified Diamond by the RIAA.
rounds.fun_fact: They started as a hardcore punk band before switching to rap after meeting Rick Rubin.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups fused jazz samples with hip-hop on their second album — creating a subgenre and releasing what many consider the greatest hip-hop album ever made. Which one?
rounds.explanation: A Tribe Called Quest's The Low End Theory (1991) pioneered jazz rap with its minimalist production, upright bass samples, and cerebral lyricism. It is consistently ranked among the greatest albums of all time.
rounds.fun_fact: Q-Tip produced nearly the entire album himself, sampling jazz legends like Art Blakey and Ron Carter.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups wrote "(You Gotta) Fight for Your Right (to Party!)" as a satire of party culture — but it was taken at face value and became a genuine frat-boy anthem. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Beastie Boys wrote the song as a parody of mindless party tracks. It reached #7 on the Hot 100 and became their only top-10 hit. They later expressed regret that the satire was lost.
rounds.fun_fact: Ad-Rock later said: "The fact that people didn't understand it was a joke was disappointing."

rounds.question_text: One of these groups lost a founding member to diabetes complications at age 45 — then released one final album as a tribute. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Phife Dawg (Malik Taylor) of A Tribe Called Quest died on March 22, 2016, from complications of diabetes. The group released We Got It from Here... Thank You 4 Your Service later that year as their farewell.
rounds.fun_fact: He had been battling diabetes since his 20s and received a kidney transplant in 2008.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups completely reinvented their sound across three decades — going from punk-rap to jazz-funk sampling to psychedelic instrumentals — releasing Paul's Boutique, Check Your Head, and Ill Communication. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Beastie Boys' musical evolution is unmatched in hip-hop. Paul's Boutique (1989) was a commercial flop but a critical masterpiece. By Hello Nasty (1998) they were blending electronic, punk, and funk.
rounds.fun_fact: Paul's Boutique sampled over 300 different recordings. It could never be made today due to sample clearance costs.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups was part of a larger collective called the Native Tongues — alongside De La Soul, Jungle Brothers, and Queen Latifah — representing conscious, Afrocentric hip-hop. Which one?
rounds.explanation: A Tribe Called Quest was a core member of the Native Tongues collective, which promoted positivity, Afrocentrism, and musical experimentation in hip-hop as a counterpoint to gangsta rap.
rounds.fun_fact: The Native Tongues were hip-hop's art school movement. While N.W.A. was burning the house down, they were painting it.

rounds.question_text: One of these groups was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2012. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Beastie Boys were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2012. MCA (Adam Yauch) had died of cancer just weeks before the ceremony. Ad-Rock and Mike D accepted without him.
rounds.fun_fact: Yauch's will stipulated that Beastie Boys' music can never be used in advertising. The group is permanently retired.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands recorded the most-streamed song from the 20th century — surpassing 1.6 billion streams globally. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody" became the most-streamed song of the 20th century in 2018, surpassing Nirvana's "Smells Like Teen Spirit" and Guns N' Roses' "Sweet Child o' Mine."
rounds.fun_fact: The song is 5 minutes 55 seconds long. Radio stations initially refused to play it because it was too long. It hit #1 anyway.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands released Back in Black — the second best-selling album in music history with over 50 million copies sold. Which one?
rounds.explanation: AC/DC's Back in Black (1980) has sold over 50 million copies, behind only Michael Jackson's Thriller. It was recorded as a tribute to their original singer who had died months earlier.
rounds.fun_fact: Only Thriller has sold more. Back in Black outsold every Beatles album, every Elvis album, and every other rock album ever made.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands lost their original lead singer to acute alcohol poisoning at age 33 — then replaced him and immediately recorded their greatest album. Which one?
rounds.explanation: AC/DC's Bon Scott died on February 19, 1980, after a night of heavy drinking in London. He was 33. The band recruited Brian Johnson and recorded Back in Black five months later.
rounds.fun_fact: The album's all-black cover was a tribute to Scott. No text, no images — just black.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands delivered what was voted the greatest live performance in rock history — a 21-minute set at Wembley Stadium in 1985. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Queen's Live Aid performance on July 13, 1985, was voted the greatest live rock performance ever in a 2005 poll. Freddie Mercury's call-and-response with 72,000 people became "The Note Heard Round the World."
rounds.fun_fact: They hadn't rehearsed for months. Mercury just walked out and owned 72,000 people in seconds.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands has a guitarist famous for performing in a schoolboy uniform — complete with shorts, blazer, tie, and a satchel — at every single show for over 50 years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Angus Young has worn his schoolboy uniform at every AC/DC concert since the mid-1970s. His sister originally suggested the outfit. He also does a signature move called the "duckwalk" across the stage.
rounds.fun_fact: He tried other costumes early on — including Spider-Man and a gorilla suit — but the schoolboy stuck.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands recorded a song that shifts between ballad, opera, and hard rock — with no chorus — and it became a #1 hit twice, 16 years apart. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Queen's "Bohemian Rhapsody" hit #1 in 1975 and again in 1991 after Freddie Mercury's death and its appearance in Wayne's World. No other song has reached #1 in two different decades.
rounds.fun_fact: The operatic section required 180 overdubs. The tape was worn nearly transparent from re-recording.

rounds.question_text: One of these bands has sold an estimated 300 million records worldwide. The other has sold 200 million. Who sold 300 million?
rounds.explanation: Queen has sold an estimated 300 million records worldwide. AC/DC has sold approximately 200 million. Both are among the top 10 best-selling music acts of all time.
rounds.fun_fact: Combined, these two bands have sold half a billion records. And people say rock is dead.

rounds.question_text: One of them recorded "I Feel Love" (1977) — a track built entirely on synthesizers that invented electronic dance music and changed pop production forever. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Donna Summer and producer Giorgio Moroder created "I Feel Love" using a Moog synthesizer. Brian Eno reportedly played it for David Bowie and said: "This is the sound of the future."
rounds.fun_fact: The track dispensed with live instruments entirely. Every sound except Summer's voice was electronic. In 1977, that was unthinkable.

rounds.question_text: One of them had the first #1 single in US history to feature rap vocals — in 1981, years before rap went mainstream. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Debbie Harry and Blondie's "Rapture" hit #1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in March 1981. It was also the first rap video aired on MTV. Harry rapped the verses herself.
rounds.fun_fact: She name-checks Fab Five Freddy and Grandmaster Flash in the lyrics — bridging downtown punk and uptown hip-hop.

rounds.question_text: One of them recorded "Love to Love You Baby" — a 17-minute track featuring simulated orgasms that was banned by the BBC and became a disco anthem. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Donna Summer recorded the extended version of "Love to Love You Baby" at Giorgio Moroder's request. It featured an estimated 22 simulated orgasms. The BBC banned it. It hit #2 in the US.
rounds.fun_fact: She recorded the vocals lying on the studio floor with the lights dimmed. Moroder kept the tape rolling.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a regular at CBGB alongside the Ramones and Talking Heads — the epicenter of New York punk. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Debbie Harry and Blondie were a CBGB house band from 1974. The club on the Bowery incubated the punk and new wave movement. Harry made it glamorous.
rounds.fun_fact: CBGB stood for "Country, Bluegrass, and Blues" — which is almost nothing like what was actually played there.

rounds.question_text: One of them won five Grammy Awards across her career and was the first artist to have three consecutive double albums reach #1 on the Billboard 200. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Donna Summer won five Grammys and made chart history with three consecutive double albums at #1: Live and More, Bad Girls, and On the Radio. No artist had done that before.
rounds.fun_fact: She was so dominant in the late 1970s that she was called the "Queen of Disco" — a title no one has ever challenged.

rounds.question_text: One of them crossed from punk to disco to rap on a single album — bridging genres that were supposedly at war with each other. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Blondie's Parallel Lines (1978) and Eat to the Beat (1979) fused punk energy with disco hooks. Then "Rapture" (1981) added rap. Harry proved genres were boundaries only for people without imagination.
rounds.fun_fact: Punk purists hated "Heart of Glass" for being too disco. Harry didn't care. It sold millions.

rounds.question_text: One of them holds the title "Queen of Disco" — the definitive voice of the biggest musical movement of the 1970s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Donna Summer is universally known as the Queen of Disco. Her hits — "Hot Stuff," "Bad Girls," "Last Dance," "On the Radio" — defined the era. She sold over 100 million records worldwide.
rounds.fun_fact: She died on May 17, 2012, at age 63, from lung cancer. She was a non-smoker — she believed the cancer came from inhaling toxic particles during 9/11.

rounds.question_text: One of them is considered the first African American woman to headline an action film — kicking down doors years before Sigourney Weaver or Linda Hamilton. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pam Grier headlined Coffy (1973) and Foxy Brown (1974), becoming the first Black woman to carry an action movie. She did it a decade before mainstream Hollywood caught up.
rounds.fun_fact: Quentin Tarantino called her "the first female action star" — and wrote Jackie Brown (1997) specifically for her.

rounds.question_text: One of them became such a powerful symbol of sexual liberation in 1950s France that her initials alone — two letters — were enough to identify her worldwide. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Bardot — known simply as "BB" — became the global symbol of French sexuality after And God Created Woman (1956). The film scandalized audiences and made her the most famous woman in Europe.
rounds.fun_fact: She was so famous that the French town of Saint-Tropez became a tourist destination solely because she lived there.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in Coffy (1973) — playing a nurse who goes on a violent vigilante rampage against drug dealers who got her little sister hooked on heroin. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pam Grier starred as Coffy, a one-woman army against the drug trade. The film was a huge box office hit and established the blaxploitation action heroine template.
rounds.fun_fact: She did many of her own stunts. The fight scenes were raw and brutal by 1970s standards.

rounds.question_text: One of them quit acting entirely to dedicate her life to animal rights — founding a foundation that has fought against seal hunting, bullfighting, and fur trapping for over 40 years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Bardot retired from acting in 1973 at age 39 and founded the Brigitte Bardot Foundation in 1986. She has spent more years fighting for animals than she ever spent in cinema.
rounds.fun_fact: She auctioned off her jewelry and personal belongings to fund the foundation. She chose animals over glamour.

rounds.question_text: One of them had their career revived by Quentin Tarantino, who wrote a starring role specifically for her in a 1997 crime film. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tarantino wrote Jackie Brown (1997) as a love letter to Pam Grier and 1970s blaxploitation cinema. The role earned her a Screen Actors Guild nomination and proved she could carry a film 25 years later.
rounds.fun_fact: Tarantino said he grew up watching her films and always wanted to make a movie with her. Jackie Brown was his chance.

rounds.question_text: One of them was so iconic in the 1950s that the French government used her face as the model for Marianne — the national symbol of the French Republic. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Bardot served as the model for Marianne, the bust that appears in every French town hall and is the national personification of France. She was chosen for her beauty and cultural significance.
rounds.fun_fact: Other women who later served as Marianne include Catherine Deneuve and Laetitia Casta.

rounds.question_text: One of them defined an entire film genre — blaxploitation — that put Black heroes at the center of action cinema for the first time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pam Grier is the defining star of blaxploitation cinema. Her films — Coffy, Foxy Brown, Sheba Baby, Friday Foster — created the template for the Black female action hero.
rounds.fun_fact: The genre was criticized for stereotypes but celebrated for putting Black faces on movie posters and in leading roles for the first time.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the first Black woman on the cover of French Vogue — and she was only 18 years old. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell appeared on the cover of French Vogue in 1988 at age 18, breaking a major barrier in fashion. She went on to become one of the original "Big Six" supermodels of the 1990s.
rounds.fun_fact: She was discovered at 15 while shopping in Covent Garden, London.

rounds.question_text: One of them married Sylvester Stallone after meeting him on the set of Rocky IV — a marriage that lasted less than two years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Nielsen married Stallone on December 15, 1985, after appearing in Rocky IV as Ludmilla, Ivan Drago's wife. They divorced in July 1987. She later said Hollywood blacklisted her after the split.
rounds.fun_fact: She was 6'1" and towered over most of her co-stars. In heels, she was taller than Stallone.

rounds.question_text: One of them became notorious for throwing a cell phone at her maid — an assault charge that followed her for years and spawned endless tabloid coverage. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell pleaded guilty to misdemeanor assault in 2007 for throwing her phone at a maid during a dispute over missing jeans. She was ordered to do community service and anger management.
rounds.fun_fact: It wasn't an isolated incident. She has multiple assault charges involving phones, BlackBerries, and other objects thrown at staff.

rounds.question_text: One of them played a Bond-adjacent villain — starring as the imposing Ludmilla Drago in Rocky IV and as the warrior Red Sonja in her own sword-and-sorcery film. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Nielsen played Ludmilla in Rocky IV (1985) and starred in Red Sonja (1985) alongside Arnold Schwarzenegger. Both roles capitalized on her striking 6'1" Amazonian presence.
rounds.fun_fact: Red Sonja flopped so badly that Schwarzenegger later called it the worst film he ever made.

rounds.question_text: One of them has a runway walk so distinctive and powerful that designers have said it can make or break a fashion show. Versace once called her "the greatest catwalk model of all time." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell's runway walk is considered the gold standard in fashion. Her fierce, confident stride has defined runway culture for over three decades. Gianni Versace specifically designed shows around her presence.
rounds.fun_fact: She has famously fallen on the runway twice in platform shoes — and turned both falls into iconic moments.

rounds.question_text: One of them was born in Denmark, stood 6'1", and used her towering physical presence to break into Hollywood as an action star in the 1980s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Nielsen was born in Rodovre, Denmark, in 1963. At 6'1", she was taller than most leading men. She parlayed her striking appearance and Scandinavian accent into an 80s action career.
rounds.fun_fact: Before acting, she was a model in Italy and Germany. Stallone spotted her in a magazine and cast her in Beverly Hills Cop II — before they even met.

rounds.question_text: One of them was born in Streatham, South London, and became one of the most famous faces on the planet — as famous for her temper as for her beauty. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell was born in South London in 1970. She has been on over 500 magazine covers and has walked for every major fashion house. Her career has spanned four decades and shows no sign of stopping.
rounds.fun_fact: She is as famous for her philanthropy — including work with Nelson Mandela's children's charity — as she is for throwing phones.

rounds.question_text: One of them was murdered at age 26 while eight and a half months pregnant — killed by members of Charles Manson's cult in one of the most horrific crimes in American history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Sharon Tate was murdered on August 9, 1969, at her home in Benedict Canyon, Los Angeles. Four others were also killed. Manson had instructed his followers to "totally destroy" everyone inside.
rounds.fun_fact: Her husband, director Roman Polanski, was away filming in London. He never fully recovered from the loss.

rounds.question_text: One of them became such a powerful symbol of French sexuality that the government used her face as the model for Marianne — the national personification of the French Republic. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Bardot served as the model for Marianne, the bust that appears in every French town hall. She was chosen for her beauty and cultural significance in the late 1960s.
rounds.fun_fact: Other women who later served as Marianne include Catherine Deneuve and Laetitia Casta.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in Valley of the Dolls (1967) — a Hollywood melodrama about the dark side of fame that became a cult classic. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Sharon Tate played Jennifer North in Valley of the Dolls, a role that showcased her beauty but frustrated her desire to be taken seriously as an actress.
rounds.fun_fact: She also starred in The Fearless Vampire Killers (1967), directed by Polanski. They fell in love on set.

rounds.question_text: One of them caused a national scandal in France with the film And God Created Woman (1956) — a movie so sexually provocative it was condemned by the Catholic Church. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Brigitte Bardot became a global sex symbol overnight with And God Created Woman, directed by Roger Vadim. The film was banned in several countries and condemned by the Vatican.
rounds.fun_fact: She was 21 years old. The film made the town of Saint-Tropez a tourist destination solely because she was associated with it.

rounds.question_text: One of them represented the loss of 1960s innocence — her murder marking the symbolic end of the flower-power era and the beginning of a darker decade. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Sharon Tate's murder in August 1969 is widely cited as the event that ended the optimism of the 1960s. Coming weeks after Woodstock, it shattered the dream of peace and love.
rounds.fun_fact: Prosecutor Vincent Bugliosi wrote Helter Skelter about the case. It became the best-selling true crime book in history.

rounds.question_text: One of them quit acting at 39 to dedicate the rest of her life to animal rights — founding a foundation that has fought against seal hunting, fur trapping, and bullfighting for over 40 years. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bardot retired from acting in 1973 and founded the Brigitte Bardot Foundation in 1986. She auctioned off her jewelry to fund it. She has spent more years fighting for animals than she ever spent in cinema.
rounds.fun_fact: She once said: "I gave my beauty and my youth to men. I am going to give my wisdom and my experience to animals."

rounds.question_text: One of them never got the chance to fulfill her potential — killed at 26 with her entire career ahead of her. Her unborn son was also lost. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Sharon Tate was 26 and eight and a half months pregnant when she was murdered. She had appeared in only a handful of films. The world never got to see what she might have become.
rounds.fun_fact: In 2019, Quentin Tarantino reimagined her story in Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, giving her the happy ending reality denied.

tagline: East Coast Lover vs West Coast Smoker

rounds.question_text: One of them chose a stage name that stands for "Ladies Love Cool James." Which one?
rounds.explanation: LL Cool J — born James Todd Smith — created the name at age 16. He said he wanted something that conveyed confidence and swagger.
rounds.fun_fact: He recorded his first demo in his grandparents' basement in Queens at age 16. It got him signed to Def Jam Records.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a debut album that entered the Billboard 200 at #1 with 806,000 copies sold in its first week — a record at the time. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg's Doggystyle (1993) debuted at #1 with 806,000 first-week sales. It featured "Gin and Juice" and "What's My Name?"
rounds.fun_fact: He recorded much of it while facing a murder charge. He was acquitted in 1996.

rounds.question_text: One of them made a comeback with "Mama Said Knock You Out" (1990) after critics had declared his career over — winning a Grammy and proving everyone wrong. Which one?
rounds.explanation: LL Cool J's fourth album Mama Said Knock You Out was a response to critics who said he'd gone soft. The title track won the Grammy for Best Rap Solo Performance.
rounds.fun_fact: He said his grandmother told him: "Oh baby, just knock them out." He named the album after her advice.

rounds.question_text: One of them became best friends with Martha Stewart — co-hosting a cooking show, attending the Olympics together, and becoming America's most unlikely celebrity duo. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg and Martha Stewart have co-hosted Martha & Snoop's Potluck Dinner Party and attended the 2024 Paris Olympics together. Their friendship started on her show in 2008.
rounds.fun_fact: Nobody predicted that a gangsta rapper and a convicted felon/homemaking queen would become inseparable. But here we are.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in the TV show NCIS: Los Angeles for 13 seasons — playing Special Agent Sam Hanna. Which one?
rounds.explanation: LL Cool J starred in NCIS: Los Angeles from 2009 to 2023, making him one of the longest-running African American leads in primetime network television.
rounds.fun_fact: He transitioned from rap to acting so smoothly that a generation knows him as an actor first and a rapper second.

rounds.question_text: One of them became an NBC commentator for the 2024 Paris Olympics — carrying the Olympic torch and becoming a wholesome family-friendly brand. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Snoop Dogg's transformation from gangsta rapper to Olympic commentator is one of pop culture's greatest pivots. He carried the torch, wore equestrian gear with Martha Stewart, and charmed the world.
rounds.fun_fact: He also coaches youth football in Los Angeles. Several of his players have gone on to the NFL.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the first solo hip-hop artist to headline Madison Square Garden. Which one?
rounds.explanation: LL Cool J was the first solo rapper to headline Madison Square Garden, cementing his status as one of hip-hop's first true arena acts in the late 1980s.
rounds.fun_fact: He has been making music for over 40 years. His career spans from 1984 to the present — longer than most genres last.

rounds.question_text: One of them wrote his own breakout screenplay in three days and refused to sell it unless he could star — despite being offered $350,000 for the script alone. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Stallone wrote the Rocky screenplay in three and a half days. Studios wanted a star like Robert Redford or Burt Reynolds. Stallone refused to sell unless he could play Rocky himself.
rounds.fun_fact: The film was made for $1 million and grossed $225 million. It won Best Picture at the Oscars.

rounds.question_text: One of them was rejected by Hollywood for being "too Chinese" — so he went to Hong Kong and became the biggest movie star on earth. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee was passed over for the lead in the TV series Kung Fu, which went to non-Asian actor David Carradine. Frustrated, Lee returned to Hong Kong and revolutionized martial arts cinema.
rounds.fun_fact: His first Hong Kong film broke every box office record in Asia. Hollywood came crawling back.

rounds.question_text: One of them created a franchise that spans six sequels, two spin-offs (Creed), and nearly 50 years of cinema. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Rocky franchise includes Rocky I-V, Rocky Balboa, and the Creed trilogy. Stallone played Rocky Balboa from 1976 to 2018 — a 42-year run with the same character.
rounds.fun_fact: The steps of the Philadelphia Museum of Art are still called "The Rocky Steps." There's a statue of him there.

rounds.question_text: One of them created his own martial arts philosophy — Jeet Kune Do, "The Way of the Intercepting Fist" — rejecting rigid styles in favor of adaptability. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee rejected classical martial arts systems and created Jeet Kune Do, a philosophy emphasizing efficiency, directness, and taking what works from any style.
rounds.fun_fact: He said: "Be water, my friend." It became the most quoted philosophy in martial arts history.

rounds.question_text: One of them was so broke before his breakout that he sold his dog for $40 — then bought it back for $15,000 after the film was greenlit. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Stallone sold his bull mastiff Butkus when he couldn't afford dog food. After Rocky was greenlit, he tracked down the buyer outside a 7-Eleven and paid $15,000 to get Butkus back.
rounds.fun_fact: Butkus appeared in the Rocky films. The dog that runs alongside Rocky up the Philadelphia steps is the same one he sold and repurchased.

rounds.question_text: One of them could punch so fast that cameras had to be slowed down to capture it — normal film speed couldn't register his movements. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee's punches and kicks were too fast for standard 24-frames-per-second film. Directors had to shoot at 32 fps and slow it down so audiences could actually see his movements.
rounds.fun_fact: He could perform a one-inch punch that sent grown men flying backward. It's been scientifically studied.

rounds.question_text: One of them died at 32 — and his most famous film was released six days after his death. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Bruce Lee died on July 20, 1973, from cerebral edema at age 32. Enter the Dragon premiered on July 26, six days later, and became a global phenomenon.
rounds.fun_fact: Stallone is still acting in his late 70s. Lee packed an entire legend into 32 years.

rounds.question_text: One of them has a shoe brand that generates over $5 billion in annual revenue — more than most professional sports teams are worth. Which one?
rounds.explanation: The Air Jordan brand generates over $5 billion annually for Nike. Jordan earns approximately $150 million per year from the brand — more than he ever made playing basketball.
rounds.fun_fact: Nike initially offered him $500,000. His mother told him to take the meeting. Best parental advice in sports history.

rounds.question_text: One of them refused to be drafted into the Vietnam War, was stripped of his world title, and banned from competition for three years during his prime. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Muhammad Ali refused military induction in 1967, saying "I ain't got no quarrel with them Viet Cong." He lost his heavyweight title and was banned from boxing for over three years.
rounds.fun_fact: The Supreme Court unanimously overturned his conviction in 1971. He came back and won the title twice more.

rounds.question_text: One of them went 6-for-6 in championship finals — never losing the big one — and was named Finals MVP all six times. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Michael Jordan won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls (1991-93, 1996-98) and was named Finals MVP every time. His perfect record in the Finals is unmatched.
rounds.fun_fact: He retired twice during his career and still won six titles. Imagine if he'd never left.

rounds.question_text: One of them predicted the round in which he would knock out his opponents — and was right often enough to terrify them. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ali would announce before fights: "He'll fall in five" or "In seven, he'll be in heaven." His pre-fight predictions were part psychological warfare, part genuine confidence, part showmanship.
rounds.fun_fact: He called himself "The Greatest" before anyone else did. Eventually, the world agreed.

rounds.question_text: One of them played through severe food poisoning in a championship game — scoring 38 points before collapsing into a teammate's arms. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Jordan's "Flu Game" in the 1997 NBA Finals against Utah is one of the most legendary performances in sports. He scored 38 points including the go-ahead three with 25 seconds left.
rounds.fun_fact: It was later revealed to be food poisoning from a late-night pizza delivery, not the flu.

rounds.question_text: One of them defeated a seemingly unbeatable champion in the "Rumble in the Jungle" — using a strategy called "rope-a-dope" to exhaust his opponent before knocking him out. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ali defeated George Foreman in Kinshasa, Zaire, on October 30, 1974. He leaned against the ropes absorbing Foreman's punches until Foreman tired, then knocked him out in round 8.
rounds.fun_fact: The fight was watched by an estimated 1 billion people worldwide. Ali was a 4-to-1 underdog.

rounds.question_text: Both are considered the greatest of all time in their respective sports. One called himself "The Greatest" before anyone agreed. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Ali proclaimed himself "The Greatest" from the start of his career. It started as braggadocio. It ended as fact. Jordan let his game do the talking — both approaches worked.
rounds.fun_fact: When asked who the greatest athlete of all time was, Jordan said: "Muhammad Ali." Even Air deferred to The Greatest.

fighter_a_name: Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five

rounds.question_text: One of them released "The Message" (1982) — the first hip-hop song to address social reality instead of party lyrics, changing rap forever. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five's "The Message" was the first hip-hop record to deal with poverty, urban decay, and social injustice. "Don't push me, 'cause I'm close to the edge" became iconic.
rounds.fun_fact: Flash himself was initially against recording it. He thought it was too serious for hip-hop. He was wrong.

rounds.question_text: One of them built a legendary studio in his backyard in Kingston, Jamaica — then allegedly burned it to the ground in 1979 after covering every surface with mystical writings. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Lee "Scratch" Perry built the Black Ark studio behind his house in Kingston. It became the birthplace of dub reggae. After years of increasingly erratic behavior, the studio was destroyed by fire.
rounds.fun_fact: Whether he intentionally set the fire is debated. He later admitted to it, saying he needed to destroy what the studio had become.

rounds.question_text: One of them invented key DJ techniques — including punch phrasing, scratching, and beat juggling — turning the turntable from a playback device into a musical instrument. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grandmaster Flash pioneered turntablism in the South Bronx in the late 1970s. He developed techniques that DJs still use today, transforming the record player into an instrument.
rounds.fun_fact: He would mark records with crayons to find exact breakbeat sections. Before him, DJs just played songs.

rounds.question_text: One of them is considered the inventor of dub music — stripping songs down to bass and drums, adding echo and reverb, and creating an entirely new genre in the process. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Lee Perry pioneered dub reggae in the early 1970s, using studio effects as instruments. His production style — heavy bass, echo, reverb, and sonic experimentation — influenced everything from punk to electronic music.
rounds.fun_fact: The Clash, The Beastie Boys, and Keith Richards all sought him out. He produced Bob Marley's earliest recordings.

rounds.question_text: One of them was in the first group of hip-hop artists inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame — in 2007. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grandmaster Flash and the Furious Five were inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2007, becoming the first hip-hop act to receive the honor.
rounds.fun_fact: Jay-Z presented the award. He said they made it possible for every rapper who came after.

rounds.question_text: One of them was known for increasingly eccentric behavior — wearing mirrors, bananas, and bottle caps glued to his body, and writing mystical symbols on everything he owned. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Lee Perry's behavior grew increasingly erratic through the late 1970s and 1980s. He covered surfaces with scrawled writings, wore bizarre costumes, and claimed to channel spirits through his music.
rounds.fun_fact: Despite the madness, he remained musically brilliant. His production style has been sampled and imitated for 50 years.

rounds.question_text: One of them helped birth an entire culture — hip-hop — from block parties in the South Bronx in the late 1970s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grandmaster Flash, alongside DJ Kool Herc and Afrika Bambaataa, is one of the three founding fathers of hip-hop. His block parties in the South Bronx created the template for the culture.
rounds.fun_fact: Perry birthed dub in Kingston. Flash birthed hip-hop in the Bronx. Two neighborhoods changed the world.

rounds.question_text: Both defined Miami in the 1980s — one as a fictional drug lord, the other as a fictional cop. Whose Miami came first on screen?
rounds.explanation: Scarface was released in December 1983. Miami Vice premiered in September 1984. Pacino's Tony Montana hit Miami first by nine months.
rounds.fun_fact: Both projects were inspired by the real cocaine cowboys flooding Miami in the early 80s.

rounds.question_text: One of them made the pastel T-shirt under an Armani blazer with no socks the defining men's look of the 1980s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson's Sonny Crockett single-handedly changed men's fashion. Armani sales spiked 30% during Miami Vice's run. Johnson said he skipped socks because "it was too hot."
rounds.fun_fact: Giorgio Armani supplied the actual clothes. The show had a bigger fashion impact than most runway collections.

rounds.question_text: One of them delivered "Say hello to my little friend" before unleashing a grenade launcher — a line that became one of the most quoted in cinema history. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Al Pacino as Tony Montana in the climactic scene of Scarface (1983). The film was a box office disappointment but became a cultural monument, especially in hip-hop.
rounds.fun_fact: Pacino said it's the film he most wished he'd been Oscar-nominated for.

rounds.question_text: One of them drove a "Ferrari" on screen that was actually a fake — built on a Corvette chassis. Ferrari was so embarrassed they donated two real cars to replace it. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Miami Vice's black Daytona Spyder was a replica on a Corvette C3 chassis. Ferrari North America donated two white Testarossas on the condition the fakes be destroyed on-screen.
rounds.fun_fact: The real Testarossas were originally black — repainted white for the show's pastel aesthetic.

rounds.question_text: One of them performed an entire role in a Cuban accent so extreme that it became both iconic and widely parodied. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Pacino's Cuban accent as Tony Montana divided critics — some called it brilliant, others called it cartoonish. Either way, "Say goodnight to the bad guy" in that accent is unforgettable.
rounds.fun_fact: Cuban Americans initially protested the film for its negative portrayal. Decades later, it's embraced as a classic.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a show that pioneered the use of contemporary pop music as part of the storytelling — Phil Collins' "In the Air Tonight" played over the pilot episode. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Miami Vice was the first TV show to use current pop hits as a narrative device. The pilot's use of Phil Collins' "In the Air Tonight" during a nighttime drive became one of the most iconic scenes in TV history.
rounds.fun_fact: Creator Michael Mann's memo to the network was two words: "No earth tones." The show was built on music and color.

rounds.question_text: One of them's portrayal of a drug lord became so influential that real drug dealers modeled their homes, clothes, and lifestyle after the character. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Tony Montana became an aspirational figure in drug culture and hip-hop. Real dealers decorated their homes with Scarface posters. Rappers from Biggie to Jay-Z to Rick Ross built entire personas around the character.
rounds.fun_fact: The irony: the film was meant as a cautionary tale about the emptiness of criminal ambition. The audience missed the moral.

tagline: Miami Vice vs Beverly Hills Cop

rounds.question_text: Both played iconic 80s cops. One was an undercover detective in Miami. The other was a Detroit detective in Beverly Hills. Whose show/film came first in 1984?
rounds.explanation: Beverly Hills Cop was released in December 1984. Miami Vice premiered in September 1984. Technically Miami Vice aired first by three months, but both defined 1984.
rounds.fun_fact: Both were the #1 hit in their medium that year — Beverly Hills Cop was the top-grossing film, Miami Vice the breakout TV show.

rounds.question_text: One of them starred in the highest-grossing film of 1984 — earning $316 million worldwide. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy's Beverly Hills Cop grossed $316 million worldwide, making it the top-grossing film of 1984. It launched a franchise and cemented Murphy as the biggest comedy star in the world.
