rounds.fun_fact: The role was originally written for Sylvester Stallone. When Stallone dropped out, Murphy made it his own.

rounds.question_text: One of them had a solo pop single reach #5 on the Billboard Hot 100 — proving he could sing as well as act. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson released "Heartbeat" in 1986, which peaked at #5 on the Hot 100 and charted across Europe. He released two albums during Miami Vice's run.
rounds.fun_fact: Eddie Murphy also had a music career — his single "Party All the Time" (produced by Rick James) hit #2 in 1985.

rounds.question_text: One of them's trademark was a distinctive, contagious laugh that became as famous as any line of dialogue. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy's laugh — a high-pitched, cackling burst — became one of the most recognizable sounds in comedy. It punctuated his stand-up specials, SNL sketches, and films.
rounds.fun_fact: His stand-up specials Delirious (1983) and Raw (1987) are considered two of the greatest comedy concerts ever filmed.

rounds.question_text: One of them changed men's fashion overnight — making pastel suits, no socks, and Ray-Bans the uniform of 1980s cool. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson's Sonny Crockett look — T-shirt, unstructured blazer, no socks, loafers — became the most copied men's style of the decade. Armani supplied the actual wardrobe.
rounds.fun_fact: Murphy had his own style impact — the leather jacket and jeans of Axel Foley were the street version of 80s cool.

rounds.question_text: One of them joined Saturday Night Live at 19 and single-handedly saved the show from cancellation. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Eddie Murphy joined SNL in 1980 at age 19 after the original cast left. His characters — Gumby, Buckwheat, Mr. Robinson — revived the show's ratings and cultural relevance.
rounds.fun_fact: He was initially hired as an extra. Within weeks, he was the reason people watched.

rounds.question_text: One of them won a Golden Globe but never an Emmy despite two nominations for his iconic 80s role. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Don Johnson won the Golden Globe for Best Actor in a TV Drama in 1986 but lost both Emmy nominations (1985, 1986) for Miami Vice.
rounds.fun_fact: Murphy was nominated for an Oscar for Dreamgirls (2006) but also never won. Both men are 80s gods without the Academy's stamp.

rounds.question_text: One of them delivered what was voted the greatest live performance in rock history — commanding 72,000 people at Wembley with just a microphone and a piano. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Freddie Mercury and Queen's Live Aid set on July 13, 1985, was voted the greatest live rock performance ever. His call-and-response with the crowd is considered the peak of live entertainment.
rounds.fun_fact: Jagger also performed at Live Aid that day with the Rolling Stones. He was great. Mercury was transcendent.

rounds.question_text: One of them has been the frontman of the same band for over 60 years — still touring in his 80s. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mick Jagger has led the Rolling Stones since 1962. The band has been touring continuously for six decades. Jagger's energy on stage in his 80s defies biology.
rounds.fun_fact: Mercury's career with Queen lasted 20 years (1970-1991). Jagger has been performing for more than three times as long.

rounds.question_text: Scientists studied one of their voices and found his vocal cords vibrated at four times the speed of a typical singer, producing extraordinary power and range. Which one?
rounds.explanation: A 2016 study analyzed Mercury's voice and found his vocal cords vibrated with remarkable speed and irregularity. Despite being known as a tenor, researchers concluded he was actually a baritone.
rounds.fun_fact: Jagger is not known for vocal range. He's known for attitude. Mercury had both.

rounds.question_text: One of them was knighted by the British Crown — becoming "Sir" for services to popular music. The honor deeply angered his bandmate. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Mick Jagger was knighted in December 2003. Keith Richards was furious, calling it "ludicrous to take one of those gongs from the Establishment."
rounds.fun_fact: Mercury was never knighted. He died in 1991 before such honors were typically given to rock stars.

rounds.question_text: One of them recorded a song that shifts between ballad, opera, and hard rock — with no chorus — and it hit #1 twice, 16 years apart. Which one?
rounds.explanation: "Bohemian Rhapsody" hit #1 in 1975 and again in 1991 after Mercury's death. The operatic section required 180 overdubs. Radio stations initially refused to play it because it was too long.
rounds.fun_fact: It became the most-streamed song of the 20th century in 2018 — 43 years after its release.

rounds.question_text: One of them co-wrote "(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction" — one of the greatest rock songs ever written — after the riff came to him in a dream. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Keith Richards came up with the riff in his sleep, recording it on a bedside tape recorder. But Jagger wrote the lyrics and the vocal melody. Together, they created rock's ultimate anthem.
rounds.fun_fact: Jagger initially didn't want to release it as a single. He thought it sounded too much like a Rolling Stones parody of themselves.

rounds.question_text: One of them publicly confirmed his AIDS diagnosis only 24 hours before dying at age 45 — keeping his illness secret to protect those around him. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Freddie Mercury announced his diagnosis on November 23, 1991, and died the following evening at his home in Kensington, London. The cause was bronchial pneumonia resulting from AIDS.
rounds.fun_fact: Jagger attended the Freddie Mercury Tribute Concert at Wembley in 1992. Mercury's absence filled the stadium more than any presence could.

rounds.question_text: Both won Academy Awards for music. One became the first African American to win an Oscar in a non-acting category. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes won the Oscar for Best Original Song for "Theme from Shaft" at the 1972 ceremony. He performed it shirtless and draped in gold chains on the Academy stage.
rounds.fun_fact: Moroder won three Oscars: Best Score for Midnight Express (1978), Best Song for "Flashdance...What a Feeling" (1983), and Best Song for "Take My Breath Away" from Top Gun (1986).

rounds.question_text: One of them produced "I Feel Love" (1977) — the first purely electronic pop hit, which Brian Eno called "the sound of the future." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Giorgio Moroder produced "I Feel Love" with Donna Summer using a Moog synthesizer. Every sound except Summer's voice was electronic. In 1977, that was revolutionary.
rounds.fun_fact: Eno played it for David Bowie and said: "I have heard the sound of the future." He was right.

rounds.question_text: One of them composed the iconic wah-wah guitar theme that opens one of the coolest films of the 1970s — a theme so recognizable you can identify the movie from three notes. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes composed the "Theme from Shaft" — the wah-wah guitar, hi-hat groove, and orchestral arrangement that defined blaxploitation cinema and won the Academy Award.
rounds.fun_fact: The opening lyrics — "Who's the black private dick that's a sex machine to all the chicks? SHAFT!" — are as iconic as the music.

rounds.question_text: One of them produced the entire soundtrack for Scarface (1983) — creating the synth-heavy sonic landscape that defined Miami's cocaine era on screen. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Giorgio Moroder composed and produced the Scarface soundtrack, including "Push It to the Limit." His electronic production style perfectly matched the film's excess and neon-lit Miami aesthetic.
rounds.fun_fact: He also scored Midnight Express, American Gigolo, Flashdance, and Top Gun. The man soundtracked the entire 1980s.

rounds.question_text: One of them voiced a beloved animated character for nearly a decade — a deep-voiced school cafeteria worker who broke into seductive songs on a cartoon show. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes voiced Chef on South Park from 1997 to 2006. He left the show after it satirized Scientology, which he practiced.
rounds.fun_fact: The creators killed off Chef in a particularly brutal episode after Hayes departed. They said the character "died" because Hayes "quit."

rounds.question_text: One of them was born in the Italian Alps — in South Tyrol — and brought a European electronic sensibility to American pop music. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Giorgio Moroder was born in Ortisei, South Tyrol, Italy, in 1940. He moved to Munich and then Los Angeles, bridging European electronic experimentation with American pop.
rounds.fun_fact: At age 74, he released a new album and DJ'd at major festivals. He was still "the future" four decades later.

rounds.question_text: One of them was a key songwriter at Stax Records in Memphis — co-writing "Soul Man" and "Hold On, I'm Comin'" for Sam & Dave before becoming a solo star. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Isaac Hayes was a songwriter and session musician at Stax Records, co-writing classic soul hits with David Porter. He built the Memphis soul sound before Shaft made him a star.
rounds.fun_fact: He didn't become famous until his late 20s. Before Shaft, he was the genius behind the curtain, writing hits for everyone else.

rounds.question_text: One of them was the first Black woman on the cover of French Vogue — at just 18 years old. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell appeared on French Vogue in 1988 at age 18, breaking a barrier in the fashion industry. She went on to become one of the original supermodels of the 1990s.
rounds.fun_fact: She was discovered at 15 while shopping in Covent Garden, London.

rounds.question_text: One of them played a Bond villain with superhuman strength in A View to a Kill — and scared Roger Moore on set. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones played May Day in the 1985 Bond film. Roger Moore later said she genuinely frightened him on set. She's one of the few Bond villains who switches sides and becomes an ally.
rounds.fun_fact: She designed her character's look in collaboration with Azzedine Alaïa.

rounds.question_text: One of them became notorious for throwing cell phones at assistants — an assault pattern so consistent it became part of her public image. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell pleaded guilty in 2007 to assaulting her maid with a phone over missing jeans. It wasn't her first or last incident involving thrown objects and staff.
rounds.fun_fact: She once said about it: "It's part of my history. I take responsibility."

rounds.question_text: One of them slapped a TV host on live television because he kept turning his back during an interview. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones slapped Russell Harty on his BBC chat show in November 1980. Ratings soared. Harty later joked his obituary would read "Grace Jones Man Dead."
rounds.fun_fact: Both women are famous for their tempers. But Jones slapped on live TV in 1980 — Campbell threw phones in private.

rounds.question_text: One of them has a runway walk so powerful that Versace called her "the greatest catwalk model of all time." Which one?
rounds.explanation: Naomi Campbell's fierce, confident stride defined runway culture for three decades. Gianni Versace designed entire shows around her presence.
rounds.fun_fact: She famously fell twice on the runway in Vivienne Westwood platform shoes — and turned both falls into iconic moments.

rounds.question_text: One of them can still hula-hoop through an entire live performance in her late 70s — doing it at the Queen's Diamond Jubilee, Afropunk, and on world tours. Which one?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones hula-hoops while performing "Slave to the Rhythm" at virtually every concert. She did it at Queen Elizabeth's Diamond Jubilee in 2012 and continues into her late 70s.
rounds.fun_fact: She was born in 1948. She is still touring and performing. The hula hoop has never stopped.

rounds.question_text: When one of them was 17 years old, the other took her out dancing at the Bain Douche nightclub in Paris — along with Iman. Who was the teenager?
rounds.explanation: Grace Jones and Iman took the young Naomi Campbell out clubbing when she was just 17. Jones was already a legend. Campbell was just getting started.
rounds.fun_fact: They've maintained a relationship for decades. Campbell has called Jones an inspiration. The torch was passed at a nightclub.
